desc: Scaling-up Nutrition Programme: Development and scale up of a sustainable, government-led school nutrition programme that leverages multi-sector financing and l

The TA requested will contribute to supporting the Government to design and implement a cross-sectoral school nutrition programme, fostering collaboration between the education, health, and agriculture sectors. desc: A strategic effort to expand El Salvador’s fiscal space through innovative financing, enabling a sustainable shift in how social programmes for children are fun
full: The technical assistance aims to advance UNICEF’s engagement on innovative financing in El Salvador, with a particular focus on exploring debt-swap mechanisms that can expand fiscal space for priority social programmes benefiting children and adolescents. The support will strengthen ongoing discussions with CAF and national authorities, assess feasible financing structures, and help design mechanisms to direct debt savings toward sustainable, equity-focused social investments. By providing analytical guidance, partnership facilitation, and strategic planning support, this technical assistance seeks to translate innovative finance opportunities into concrete, country-level solutions that enhance the impact and sustainability of social programmes in El Salvador.

desc: Participation of the Reference Group for the ¨Summative and formative evaluation of the out-of-school project implemented by UNICEF Venezuela¨
full:
The Education Specialist supporting the Evaluation Reference Group plays a technical advisory role throughout all phases of the evaluation, with a particular focus on educational quality, pedagogical relevance, and the coherence of the evaluative analysis of the Learning Recovery Programme for Out-of-School Children and Adolescents.
The primary responsibility of the Education Specialist is to provide specialized expertise in education, learning recovery, and educational pathways for out-of-school children and adolescents, ensuring that the evaluation:
• It is grounded in relevant pedagogical, policy, and programmatic frameworks.
• Adequately reflects the national and regional education context.
• Produces technically sound findings, conclusions, and recommendations that are relevant and actionable for decision-making.
The Education Specialist participates as an active member of the Evaluation Reference Group, contributing to the review and discussion of key consultancy deliverables (inception report, draft evaluation reports, and recommendations), engaging in technical meetings, and supporting the interpretation of results from an education-sector perspective.
The time commitment for this support is estimated at approximately two (2) working days across all stages of the evaluation.

desc: COE Digital Expert Support for GCRI Roadmap Phase: Application Review, Transition Decisions & System Rationalization
full:
The Global Cyber Resiliency Initiative (GCRI) has completed the Assessment Phase, identifying 1,900+ digital systems across country offices, including T4D initiatives, fundraising platforms, and custom operational systems.

Assessment results reveal critical issues requiring expert evaluation during the Roadmap Phase, such as:

Fragmented system landscape; many systems built outside governance pathways.
High‑risk systems lacking maintenance contracts, proper hosting, authentication, or classification.
Need for programmatic validation to determine whether a system should be updated, transitioned, or decommissioned.

UNICEF lacks sufficient specialized digital expertise—particularly in product viability, architectural fitness, modernisation pathways, and field‑programme relevance—to ensure high‑quality decisions in the Roadmap Phase.

TAHUB / COE Digital engagement is required to ensure safe, sustainable, and programmatically aligned decisions globally.

desc: Capacity building of UNICEF Pacific staff
full:
Last year marked the midpoint of UNICEF Pacific MCPD, and we conducted a deep-dive exercise of the climate programme. One of the key findings was that many colleagues do not yet feel sufficiently equipped to integrate climate considerations into their work, which is limiting the shift of our programmes toward climate resilience. A critical enabler of success is ensuring that UNICEF Pacific staff have the skills and tools needed to support programme design, implementation and resource mobilization, enabling a sustained transition toward climate-resilient programming.

We would therefore like to request support from the COE in the design and implementation of a training programme. The initial objective is to strengthen staff understanding of the basic science of climate change and its impacts on children, social services and UNICEF programmes, as well as the key frameworks that guide climate action, including adaptation, mitigation and loss and damage. The training would also aim to build practical skills on how to design climate- and risk-informed programming. 

We expect participation from all result areas. Given that our office operates across six locations in the Pacific Islands (one multi-country office and five field offices), the training may need to be delivered through more than one session. We would also hope for the training to be highly practical and action-oriented, supporting teams to apply the concepts directly to their programme areas.

The instance will support Generation Unlimited’s focus on green skills development for youth and will host relevant training content as part of this initiative.

A full description of the request and concept is attached for additional context. This ticket is intended to initiate the technical setup of the instance.

desc: Provide technical assistance to support the integration of child-centred loss and damage considerations into national systems and UNICEF programming
full: Provide technical assistance to support the integration of child-centred loss and damage considerations into national systems and UNICEF programming. This includes developing practical guidance for identifying, documenting and analysing non-economic and economic losses affecting children (health, learning, protection, displacement, environmental harm), strengthening links between loss and damage, anticipatory action and recovery, and clarifying entry points for UNICEF within national coordination and financing processes. The support will enable country offices to move from ad-hoc shock response towards systematic, child-sensitive approaches that inform policy dialogue, recovery programming and access to emerging loss and damage financing windows.

desc: Establish a national, child-centred DRM policy embedding resilience, early warning, and climate adaptation across sectors.
full:
Module A — DRM Policy co-design & consultation (≈ 10–12 weeks) 

What CoE does: Provide policy drafting support (structure, content, inter-ministerial roles) and facilitate a concise consultative process at national and zoba levels; prepare an operationalization plan (governance, SOPs, early warning, preparedness drills, financing hooks). 

What it strengthens: A child-centred DRM Policy, validated and ready for adoption, with a clear action plan and role handshakes. 

Standards/tools: UNICEF CEED/DRR guidance; consultation playbook; operational plan templates. 

Module B — Light vulnerability profiling (2–3 zobas) 

What CoE does: Apply a rapid, indicator-based profile (hazard exposure, WASH/health/nutrition access, school resilience, protection risks); map existing assets (solar water systems, catchments, early warning practices). 

What it strengthens: Practical evidence to prioritize resilience investments and preparedness routines. 

Standards/tools: Rapid profiling toolkit; simple geotag forms; child risk lens (LNOB/HRBA). 

Module C — Programme integration: shock-resilience & climate adaptation 

What CoE does: Run short design clinics with WASH/Health/Nutrition/Education/Child Protection to embed resilience add-ons (e.g., climate-resilient WASH O&M, school safety/continuity, nutrition continuity, protection case-management contingencies). 

What it strengthens: Realistic resilience features inside ongoing activities; documented add-ons and quick wins. 

Standards/tools: Resilience add-on menu; preparedness SOPs; continuity checklists. 

Module D — Early warning & preparedness routines (lite) 

What CoE does: Set up simple early warning triggers and action protocols in pilot zobas; support quarterly preparedness drills; create a one-page dashboard for PMT/CMT. 

What it strengthens: Predictable preparedness and faster response; leadership visibility on risk status. 

Standards/tools: EW trigger table; drill playbook; dashboard template.

desc: Pilot scalable, MRV-aligned GHG measurement systems to support climate-resilient housing solutions.
full: The purpose of an IoT sensor–based GHG measurement system for insulated gers is to quantify GHG emission reductions and adaptation benefits achieved through improved insulation of traditional ger dwellings. The system enables real-time or near–real-time Monitoring, Reporting, and Verification (MRV) to support climate policy, donor reporting, and potential carbon finance mechanisms. The IoT-based MRV system combines physical sensors, data transmission, and analytics platforms to measure emissions indirectly but accurately.

The TA should include targeted capacity-building, practical tools, and implementation support to enable scalable DRR integration, including linkages with financing opportunities where relevant.

This includes targeted  review and quality assurance support from German, Tiangco and Velasquez at key milestones of the GCF PPF application and subsequent full funding proposal, ensuring strong alignment with UNICEF standards, child-centred approaches, safeguards and fund requirements. In parallel, periodic technical guidance is needed to shape and review the emerging Loss and Damage Fund concept and proposal, with a focus on child-centred early warning systems, anticipatory action and compliance with evolving fund guidance.

This includes preparing proposals for the Green Climate Fund and other donors to mobilize resources for large-scale interventions that protect children from climate impacts. TA will also help build government and partner capacity for implementation, monitoring, and accountability, ensuring resilience programming is institutionalized and delivers measurable outcomes for children and communities.

In 2026, BCO will develop a Climate and Disaster Resilience Roadmap, strengthening its evidence base and policy positioning through child-centered climate and risk analysis and the application of global and regional best practices. This will support the transformation of programming so that essential services such as education, health, WASH and social protection are climate-resilient and shock-responsive, with DRR and anticipatory systems integrated as standard practice. The support will also strengthen BCO’s climate resilience investment package to mobilize climate finance and explore innovative disaster risk financing options.

full: The technical assistance aims to support El Salvador in strategically integrating artificial intelligence (AI) across the education system to enhance learning outcomes, equity, and teacher quality. UNICEF will provide expertise in designing AI-powered tools for personalized student learning, virtual tutoring, and real-time progress monitoring, as well as innovative teacher training programs with feedback and coaching. The support includes developing data-driven decision-making platforms, adapting international best practices, and ensuring inclusive approaches for vulnerable populations. Ultimately, this technical assistance will help operationalize AI solutions, strengthen national capacity, and accelerate the country’s path toward globally competitive education outcomes and sustainable system-level transformation

The initiative aims to embed proactive, integrated approaches that safeguard children and communities against escalating climate and disaster risks.

desc: Support development of the SCAP through consultative workshops, stakeholder communication, and capturing children's voices.
full: The CO is currently in the process of developing the SCAP. The consultant is now on board and has submitted the inception report. A consultative workshop with all stakeholders is planned to be organized at the beginning of March. In addition, the CO are planning to capture the voices of children as part of this process. However, they are facing challenges in managing communication for key informant interviews, particularly with younger children and adolescents. At the national level, they are liaising with relevant experts, especially in early childhood communication. Nevertheless, they would greatly value our experience and insights on how child engagement is managed in other countries where similar approaches have been implemented. A SCAP task team representing each of UNICEF’s core thematic areas of focus has been established.

desc: Support deepening UNICEF Malaysia's climate-education programming under the new CPD 2026-2030 climate outcome.
full: Malaysia’s new CPD (2026-2030) includes a dedicated climate outcome, emphasizing the right of children and young people to live in a clean, safe, healthy, and sustainable environment, while strengthening their resilience to climate and environmental shocks. As Malaysia CO expand their climate work under this outcome, they are deepening our focus on the intersection between climate change and education, including safer schools, DRR education, and environmental education. The CO also have several exciting evidence generation initiatives underway, including the Child Friendly NDC 3.0, the National Youth Climate Change Survey, and the Children’s Climate & Disaster Risk Index. The CO would like to connect with the COE to exchange learning, synergies and share experiences from our work in this space.

desc: Review of Malawi's MAF concept note and support on carbon quantification, policy mapping, and identification of comparable large-scale projects.
full: Supported the review of Malawi’s MAF concept note and held follow-up discussions with the Country Office. The CO ultimately decided not to proceed in this round due to internal priorities. Support also included carbon quantification inputs, concept note review, mapping of relevant policy-related initiatives, and identification of comparable large-scale projects across the CO portfolio (USD 250,000+).

desc: Transaction advisory services and climate finance mobilization for Pakistan, including revenue-sharing structuring for community-based financing.
full:
UNICEF to play role for transaction advisory and climate finance mobilization. 
•Provide independent, transparent, and technically sound transaction advisory (TA) services to PSPA (Revenue sharing and credit selling) drawing on UNICEF’s global expertise, fiduciary standards, and experience in community-based financing mechanisms.
•Support the structuring of a transparent revenue-sharing arrangement between ATR and PSPA to ensure that the agreed community share of carbon revenues is securely channeled through UNICEF’s established financial and governance systems and protocols which are globally acceptable and help in improving the price of credits- can be premium credits.
•As a follow up PSPA would look forward to UNICEF playing a collaborative role in next phase with ATR Inc (UNICEF has no financial liability to get the project registered this will be on ATR) in registering additional WFP, with UNICEFs to provide transaction advisory role and play the role of project proponent to receive the monetization of CERs for reinvestment in communities (community investment share).

desc: Review GCF Accredited Entity request from the Government of Rwanda and advise on UNICEF's capacity to proceed.
full: The Government of Rwanda has once again requested UNICEF Rwanda CO to be the AE for the preparation of the proposal for GCF. This is despite the fact that the CO earlier informed them that UNICEF may not be able to do so due to (i) focus on other priority countries, and (ii) considering the fact that Rwanda has already received significant funds from GCF and the country’s current GCF pipeline is crowded. The Government, however, is of the view that Rwanda has not previously benefitted from GCF funding for clean cooking and hence the subject project stands high chances of being considered for funding.

desc: Support Sierra Leone CO on Peace Renewable Energy Credits process, governance requirements, and government engagement modalities.
full: Supporting the Country Office in discussions with government on Peace Renewable Energy Credits (P-RECs), including the process, governance requirements, and management-level briefing on the risks and opportunities of different models. This includes assessing both a UNICEF-led crediting route and a government-led route. The model is being tested in South Sudan and Ethiopia and is now also being explored in Sierra Leone.

desc: Guide the next phase of the CCRI project including government handover, findings dissemination, and sustaining momentum.
full: The SACO CCRI has been taken up by colleagues at the National Strategic Hub (NSH), which sits under the Department of Cooperative Governance and Traditional Affairs (COGTA). The contract for the consultant working on the CCRI has ended. Now we are looking at the next phase of the project and how we can facilitate a smooth handover to our colleagues at government as well as disseminate the findings appropriately at the national level. In order to manage the CCRI on GeoSight, colleagues at COGTA need access to the platform. Therefore with need some assistance from the GeoSight team in providing Gov colleagues access and to answer any questions they may have regarding hosting and updating the SA CCRI.

desc: Introduce UNICEF Turkmenistan's climate action programming under the new CPD 2026-2030 and discuss youth and green skills priorities.
full: CO would like to introduce themselves and present UNICEF Turkmenistan’s achievements to date, and share key priorities under the Country Programme Document (CPD) 2026–2030, where climate action is recognized as a strategic priority. Under the climate action and youth flagship area, the new programme of cooperation aims to significantly increase the number of children and young people equipped with climate adaptation and DRR knowledge and skills, while promoting sustainable “green” behaviours. These efforts are fully aligned with the country’s 2030 Agenda commitments, the National Climate Change Strategy, the National Action Plan for Realization of Children’s Rights (2023-2028), and the NDC. A key component is the expansion of the national “Green School” initiative — jointly launched with the Ministry of Education in 2025 — with plans to reach 500 schools by 2030. Given the ambition of the current programme cycle and current resource mobilization needs in climate action, the CO would value the opportunity to explore potential areas of alignment and discuss whether submission of a concept note under the CEED thematic pool (2026–2029 funding window) may be feasible.

desc: Technical review of the Climate Change Module and toolkits for young people under the KOICA-funded Climate Action for the Last Mile project.
full: Under the KOICA-funded Climate Action for the Last Mile project, the UNICEF Timor-Leste Social Policy team is currently developing a Climate Change Module and toolkits for young people. In February, the CoE ADAP team provided a preliminary review of the draft materials. However, we would greatly appreciate it if colleagues from the Climate Change CoE could also provide a technical review and feedback from a climate perspective before we finalize the documents.

desc: Regional study on the impacts of heat and poor air quality on children's learning and wellbeing in MENA, with policy recommendations.
full: UNICEF MENA Regional Office launched this regional study to explore the impacts of heat and poor air quality on children’s learning and wellbeing. The study gathers evidence on the impacts of heat and poor air quality on children’s wellbeing and learning, assessed existing coping and mitigation measures, identified additional actions for families, teachers, and policymakers, and outlined key channels to influence policy processes to address these risks. Surveys and FGDs completed in Iraq, Yemen and Djibuti. Now the Magenda team is involved in planning and collecting data in Qatar during february-March 2026. UNICEF SBC team will support the analysis.

desc: Support development of a concept proposal for the CIDCA Special Funding Window on South-South Cooperation for Bangladesh.
full:
Support development of a concept proposal for the CIDCA (China International Development Cooperation Agency) Special funding window under the Global Development and South-South Cooperation Fund. CIDCA has opened a new application window for development projects (March–May 2026) which is the first since 2022. Following consultations with ROSA and the China Country Office, Bangladesh has been confirmed as a ROSA priority country and has been invited to submit a concept note for funding consideration. Based on internal discussions last Thursday, the CO has indicated interest in a multi-sectoral project on climate change and gender (with a targeted focus on adolescent girls), with the proposed following components:

•Comprehensive adolescent health (SRH, menstrual hygiene, nutrition, mental health)
•Climate-resilient education and life skills
•Climate-resilient WASH (focused on the Chittagong Hill Tracts and coastal communities)
•Disaster risk reduction (DRR) and emergency preparedness
•Adolescent girls' agency and capacity building

desc: Technical support to design, configure and operationalise a digital child protection case management system (PRIMERO/CPIMS+) aligned with the Child...
full: Technical support to design, configure and operationalise a digital child protection case management system (PRIMERO/CPIMS+) aligned with the Child Protection Law and ongoing social welfare reforms. CoEs will provide guidance on system configuration, workflows, data standards, safeguarding protocols, interoperability with social protection and justice systems, and capacity-building for frontline and supervisory staff. This will enable Government to implement a robust, safe and scalable digital case management platform that strengthens accountability and service quality.

desc: Ongoing support to strengthen Uzbekistan’s national system for preventing and responding to violence against children, including GBV
full: Ongoing support to strengthen Uzbekistan’s national system for preventing and responding to violence against children, including GBV. CoEs will advise on policy and protocol development, harmonisation of intersectoral referral pathways, frontline practice standards, and workforce development models. Support will also reinforce social norms and demand-generation approaches. This routine TA will ensure that legal and policy advances translate into consistent, high-quality protection for children across sectors and levels of the system.

desc: Providing support for monitoring the implementation of treaty body recommendations

desc: Align migration programming with global compacts to strengthen protection for children on the move
full: Support the Egypt Country Office to articulate a coherent, child-centred strategy on migration programming aligned with the Global Compact for Migration and the Global Compact on Refugees. The strategy should position Egypt as a key migration hub and committed signatory, linking national strategies with global principles on safe migration, protection, inclusion, and shared responsibility. It should highlight UNICEF’s contributions across child protection, social protection, and education, demonstrating the humanitarian-development nexus and inclusion of migrants and refugees in national systems. A unified narrative will strengthen advocacy, partnerships, programme coherence, and resource mobilization, while reinforcing Egypt’s regional leadership in migration governance.

desc: The strategic purpose of this request is to lay the foundation for integrated VAC prevention and response programme management
full: The strategic purpose of this request is to lay the foundation for integrated VAC prevention and response programme management. Addressing VAC in BiH requires moving beyond fragmented, sectoral, project-based interventions. Thus support is needed to establish programme management structure within the CO with identifying entry points, including catalytic policy and financing. The goal is to shift from siloed interventions to integrated, system-wide programming. UNICEF BiH seeks support in designing and operationalizing VAC related interventions, including programme design for high impact violence prevention solutions. CoE support is requested to conduct a scoping analysis of VAC policy environment, coordination structures, and delivery mechanisms. The TA would guide the design of a cross sectoral operational framework, define roles, propose budget lines and financing options, and develop a monitoring and learning agenda. Special attention should be given to integrating VAC work with the implementation of the European Child Guarantee, de-institutionalization reform, educational, and juvenile justice programmes. This technical support will be very timely also in light of the planned recruitment of a Child Protection Specialist in January 2026 in laying the groundwork for a more cohesive and strategic VAC portfolio, addressing current fragmentation and enabling UNICEF to engage holistically in this critical area

desc: Technical assistance for ECG planning, implementation, and evaluation in BiH by supporting and advising on governance, institutional, and technical capacities
full: Technical assistance for ECG planning, implementation, and evaluation in BiH by supporting and advising on governance, institutional, and technical capacities. Support quantitative & qualitative deep dive analyses on child poverty, social exclusion,  service access and barriers, including child health services and health-related barriers, and support prioritization of measures. Promote child and youth participation through inclusive consultations, peer-learning, and mentoring. Support country capacities for future EU policies, statistics, and funding (EaSI, ESF, etc). The support has been provided by ECARO covering EU Accession countries, including BiH. BiH is planned to pay a visit to Croatia with other Western Balkan Accession Countries on ECG

The programme requires periodic technical support to inform design and sustain implementation. 
For Child Protection sector coordination, this includes validating survey results on localization and supporting the development of a related action plan, as well as supporting the drafting of the annual report. For social workforce strengthening and children's care reform, support is needed to participate in consultations, organize study tours, and review documents. On the integration of child protection and social policy, inputs are required to build the case management system for Cash++. For MRM/CAAC, short-term assistance is needed to organize and deliver a training-of-trainers on the MRM. Further technical support might be required to strengthen responses for children on the move. This would include guidance for the development of standardized cross-border case management mechanisms, ensuring coherent procedures.

desc: Strengthen national capacities to prevent and respond to digital violence within child protection systems

desc: Transform juvenile justice systems to prioritize prevention, diversion, and rehabilitation through child-sensitive, rights-based approaches
full: Technical assistance is required to re-design and institutionalize a child-sensitive juvenile justice framework that embeds prevention, diversion, and rehabilitation as core principles. This support will help develop national standards, operational guidelines, and inter-agency coordination mechanisms to replace punitive approaches with restorative practices. It will also guide the creation of structured diversion programs, community-based rehabilitation services, and capacity-building plans for justice and social welfare actors. By integrating these reforms into policy and practice, the assistance will deliver a sustainable system that protects children’s rights, reduces recidivism, and strengthens cross-sector collaboration for holistic child protection.

desc: Institutionalize integrated family care systems and strengthen the social workforce to reduce reliance on institutional care
full: Technical assistance is required to design and operationalize a national framework for family care systems and social workforce strengthening. This support will help develop policies and standards for family-based care, including parenting programs, caregiver support, and family-friendly workplace policies, while reducing reliance on institutional care. It will also guide the creation of a social service workforce strategy, including competency frameworks, training plans, and financing models to professionalize and expand the workforce. By embedding these reforms into national systems, the assistance will deliver sustainable mechanisms for child protection, family resilience, and integrated social services across Maldives.

desc: Technical support to roll out the National Action Plan on Prevention and Response to Violence Against Children, including guidelines, training, and monitoring.
full:
3. Supporting the Rollout of the National Action Plan on Prevention and Response to Violence Against Children

Short-term technical support is required to facilitate the rollout of the National Action Plan on Prevention and Response to Violence Against Children (VAC). This includes reviewing implementation guidelines and training materials, providing targeted inputs to strengthen coordination mechanisms, and troubleshooting operational challenges during rollout. Assistance will also involve technical review of monitoring tools, periodic feedback on progress reports, and guidance on integrating VAC prevention into existing child protection and education platforms. These practical, day-to-day interventions will ensure timely implementation and alignment with national standards and UNICEF’s child protection priorities.

desc: High-level advocacy and legal review to safeguard child rights, including draft law analysis and standards on minimum age of criminal responsibility.
full:
6. Child Rights Advocacy: Safeguarding Legal Standards and Policy Commitments for Adolescents

Technical support is required to provide high-level advocacy and legal review to safeguard child rights amid policy backsliding risks. This includes reviewing draft laws and policy proposals, preparing advocacy briefs, and providing technical advice on international standards related to minimum age of criminal responsibility and adolescent rights. Assistance will also involve developing evidence-based arguments, troubleshooting legislative gaps, and supporting UNICEF’s engagement with government and civil society to prevent harmful measures such as lowering the age of criminal responsibility or creating isolated facilities for adolescents. These targeted inputs will sustain ongoing advocacy and protect child rights commitments.

• Building capacity of the CP team for sustainable financing through improved public financial management and investment frameworks to scale essential services while focusing on information system data quality and utilization. 
• Additionally, technical assistance will drive knowledge generation through policy briefs, technical notes, and implementation research to inform evidence-based decision-making. Ad hoc support will address emerging priorities such as thematic guidance, resource mobilization, and access to a consultant roster.

desc: Evaluate and renew national strategies to end child marriage through evidence-based, system-level reform
full: UNICEF Madagascar is requesting technical assistance to support the evaluation of the National Strategy on Ending Child Marriage (2017–2024). The implementation of the strategy faced a number of challenges, making a thorough evaluation essential before the development of a new strategy. During this period, the prevalence of girls aged 20–24 who were married before the age of 18 increased from 39% in 2018 to 42% in 2025, underscoring the need for sustained and strengthened national action. This includes continued multisectoral engagement and, based on the findings of the evaluation, the development of a new national strategy, with the mobilisation of key partners, including UNFPA.

desc: Scale justice for children from pilot initiatives to a national system
full: UNICEF is currently implementing an integrated service package for justice for children in one major urban area (Kandy). By end-Q1 2026, the first six months of implementation of this pilot will provide operational evidence to assess functionality, gaps, and system-readiness for expansion. UNICEF will need to assess and use that evidence to propose a cost-efficient pathway for sustainable scale-up nationwide. This will likely include adjusting institutional responsibilities, refining the model based on early lessons, and developing a scalable financing and workforce approach. The specifics—appropriate sequencing, cost implications, and political feasibility—will become clearer once early performance data and feedback is analysed with UNICEF’s aim being to ensure that a realistic, evidence-based scale-up plan is on the table for government consideration by Q2/Q3 to feed into the preparation of the 2027 budget planning process.

This was the major innovation of this CPD (National Conference on Development), which is to treat the specialized child protection system as a system that must have established pathways, protocols, guidelines, etc. It would be a way to successfully complete what we have started regarding specialized protection services, something that has not been done before in the country.

desc: Institutionalize digital birth registration across social sectors to ensure universal legal identity for children in Bangladesh
full: Technical assistance is required to design and operationalize a national digital birth registration framework. This includes system architecture and interoperability with health and social protection platforms, legal and policy updates, and robust data protection measures. Support will cover last-mile service design, workforce training, and change management to ensure adoption at scale. TA will also guide financing strategies and sustainability planning, enabling government-led rollout and monitoring for coverage, equity, and timeliness. These reforms will institutionalize universal birth registration and strengthen linkages to health and social protection systems.

desc: Operationalize government-led programmes to prevent violence against girls, boys, and women using trauma-informed approaches
full:
TA to key stakeholders (health, education, social protection, justice) to develop, implement and monitor behaviorally and trauma-informed strategies and support interventions and awareness campaigns in ending all forms of violence against girls, boys and women (VAGBW) that focus on preventing physical, emotional, and sexual abuse by promoting safe environments at home, in schools, and online.
Kosovo currently lacks behaviorally informed and trauma informed strategies and operational tools for preventing VABGW at home, in schools and online. Despite the high prevalence of VAGBW, institutions across sectors (education, health, social welfare, justice, municipalities) have limited capacities to design, implement and monitor such approaches.
Technical Assistance is required to: 
1. Support the development of a multi-sectoral behaviorally and trauma informed VAGBW prevention Strategy.
2. Create implementation guidance, minimum standards, framework and monitoring tools. 
3. Strengthen institutional capacities to apply, scale and sustain these approaches across sectors in routine practice.

desc: Provide technical assistance focused on institutional and workforce support to operationalize the new Children, Adolescents and Youth Code by supporting the...
full:
Provide technical assistance focused on institutional and workforce support to operationalize the new Children, Adolescents and Youth Code by supporting the establishment of a national child protection governing body and translating this framework into actionable local governance and development programmes. 

This includes reviewing draft structures, advising on intersectoral coordination mechanisms, and sharing international experiences on law implementation to strengthen national level and municipal child protection systems.

desc: The CO is aiming to work with the government and convene strategic financing partners such as ADB, World Bank, etc, to put child protection system financing...
full: The CO is aiming to work with the government and convene strategic financing partners such as ADB, World Bank, etc, to put child protection system financing and reforms high on the agenda. The CO will require support in framing this convening, identifying key policy / advocacy documents and other documentation that supports the financing agenda. The CO does not yet have a date for this convening. However, the COs estimates and aims for April / May 2026. In addition to supporting the lead up, the CO is asking for in-person support for the meeting itself, where the senior technical advisor would play a role in the proceeding, including liaising where relevant with development partners in their respective HQs and ROs, and support engagement with the different stakeholders on the sidelines of the discussions. Ensure the networking and experience exchange opportunities with different countries on operationalizing the corporal punishment ban in a global or regional/subregional context.

desc: The Government has just made a major breakthrough based on UNICEF advocacy and high level in country missions to put the Prosecutor General's office in the...
full: The Government has just made a major breakthrough based on UNICEF advocacy and high level in country missions to put the Prosecutor General's office in the lead, coordinating the development of a national programme on child protection and justice. 2026 will be an instrumental year to support the development of that national programme. Senior Technical assistance is required to support the CO with national partners on strengthening key components of that natioanl programme, ensuring that it has a strong basis in family-based alternative care, expansion and strengthening of the social service workforce, strengthening special measures for children in conflict and contact with the law, etc. Support is needed to facilitate consultations, provide and commuinicate global and regional evidence, and build a shared understanding among ministries on the need to gradually shift from institutional care toward family- and community-based support across the child protection and justice systems. The CO will be working with the government to identify the strategic moment to have these face to face meetings and convenings. It is anticipated that it will take place May.

desc: Strengthen digital public infrastructure to deliver universal birth registration and lifetime legal identity
full:
The technical assistance includes an initial ecosystem analysis and a mapping, in collaboration with the CO, to understand the roles and coordination mechanisms of key partners and to identify UNICEF’s entry and engagement areas.

It will then support the development of Malawi’s national DPI vision and roadmap and outline how a lifetime unique ID can link individuals to essential social services. Strategic engagement with the e-Government department on these areas may also be included.

full: Technical assistance is required to design and implement the digitization of Risiha tools, including technical specifications, integration with provincial and national IMS, data standards, workflows, and quality assurance. Additional support is needed to guide the government-led pathway to ownership of ParentText/ParentApp, covering governance structures, content stewardship, costed implementation plans, data protection protocols, and long-term systems integration. This assistance will build national capacity to sustain and scale both platforms, ensuring they function as core components of child protection systems for prevention and early intervention.

desc: 1) Routine technical support is required throughout 2026 to sustain digitised Risiha tools and government-managed ParentText/ParentApp, while building...
full:
1) Routine technical support is required throughout 2026 to sustain digitised Risiha tools and government-managed ParentText/ParentApp, while building in-house capacity for long-term ownership. This includes troubleshooting, refining data workflows, resolving IMS integration issues, and providing rapid inputs on content updates, analytics, quality assurance, and data protection.  Additional support is needed to develop a TOR for a UNICEF resource to oversee RapidPro and global digital parenting initiatives, defining governance roles, quality standards, and facilitating co-creation for scalable integration into government digital service delivery systems.
2) Birth registration estimates- South Africa 's statistics bureau is struggling to get a proper birth registration data- both through census and admin data. Few Academic Institutions (such as Children's Insitute ) has been trying to establish the model to estimate birth registration rate, but it has been a struggle. if UNICEF's data expert can provide in the methods of estimation, it can support the evidence generation and evidence-based policy level advocacy. 
3) Southern Africa migration coordination support- following the MIDSA in 2025, there has been Ministerial declaration adopted on Children on the Move, however, the practical coordination for case management between countries had been weak due to the lack of practical SOPs or focused coordination mechanism. It could be useful to have regional level coordination support between relevant countries, so there is a coherent SOP adopted across relevant SADC countries.

desc: Shift from fragmented projects to a government-led national programme to end violence against children
full:
Technical assistance to strengthen Gabon’s national child protection system should focus on:
(1) Developing practical strategies and operational guidelines to ensure the effective implementation of child protection laws and policies, translating legal frameworks into actionable programmes at all levels;
(2) Designing and rolling out evidence-based strategies to mobilize communities, raise awareness, and shift harmful social norms, including participatory approaches and capacity building for local leaders; and
(3) Providing advice on resource mobilization, including guidance on sustainable financing, budgeting, and building partnerships with donors, the private sector, and civil society for long-term programme sustainability.

desc: Prevent harmful and illegal behavior through integrated legal, identification, and early support systems
full: Technical assistance is required to revise the existing legal initiative on children below MACR, ensuring alignment with international standards and child rights principles. This includes assistance in designing a coherent system of prevention and support mechanisms to address difficult and illegal behaviors among children under the criminal age threshold—such as early identification, family support services, community-based interventions, and coordinated responses across social, educational, and child protection sectors.

desc: Reduce violence against children by aligning protection, MHPSS, emergency response, and SBC within national systems
full:
1. Violence Against Children: Review and strengthen national VAC and MHPSS policies, align legislation with regional standards, and support the design of integrated multisector protocols and referral pathways.
2. Child Protection in Emergencies: Develop and refine emergency preparedness and response procedures, coordination mechanisms and training tools to ensure consistent protection of children during crises.
3. Social Behaviour Change & Social Norms: Design evidence-based SBC strategies, messaging and community engagement tools that shift harmful norms and promote positive caregiving to improve children’s safety and wellbeing.

desc: Strengthen Namibia’s child protection system through legal reform, digital case management, and multisector service delivery
full:
1. Provide high-level policy, legal drafting, and coordination support to finalize and operationalize the Cybercrime Bill (integration of OCSEA) and National Child Protection Policy to strengthen Namibia’s legal and policy environment, embed global child protection standards, and establish clear, enforceable frameworks for prevention, reporting and accountability.
2. TA to advance the rollout, integration, and interoperability of the electronic Case Management System (eCMS), data protection protocols, system architecture guidance, and analytics dashboards that enable real-time, evidence-based case monitoring and decision-making to standardize data flows and child-sensitive information management across all regions.
3. Design and operationalize scalable program models for child protection in migration contexts, MHPSS integration into community and school systems, and emergency mitigation for VAC/GBV, embedded within multisectoral structures under the CPD and Strategic Plan.

desc: Support identifying donor funding opportunities and preparing qualitative studies on topics such as adolescent pregnancy, early unions, and child labour.
full:
1. Support with the identification of funding opportunities, either for Bolivia or as part of multi-country proposals. In this regard, the level of effort and timing will depend on the opportunities identified by the CoE. The support requested focuses on identifying and engaging with donors. 
2. Preparation of studies with a more qualitative focus (causes and factors) and less quantitative (data and statistics). The specific theme is left to the CoE’s suggestion, as the expectation is for a regional, sub-regional, or multi-country study. Some possible themes include: adolescent pregnancy, early unions, sexual violence, and child labor.

desc: A Joint mission between RO (CP and HARP) and UNICEF Tanzania CO (Emergency, KFO and CP) to the refugee camps (Nduta and Nyarugusu) in Kigoma region
full: A Joint mission between RO (CP and HARP) and UNICEF Tanzania CO (Emergency, KFO and CP) to the refugee camps (Nduta and Nyarugusu) in Kigoma region. The aim of the visit is to assess the current situation of refugee children in the two refugee camps in Nduta (due for official closure in March  2026)and Nyarugusu  (hosting both refugees from Burundi (official closure in June 2026) and the DRC), and provide technical guidance on how to ensure a safe and dignified return of Burundian children as well as sustaining the basic servioces for the remaining Congolese refugees in Nyarugusu and the residual caseload of Burundian refugees.

Technical assistance is required to leverage regional knowledge and evidence on diversion and non-custodial measures.

desc: Operationalize the European Child Guarantee to ensure inclusive access to services for vulnerable children
full:
Montenegro requires targeted technical assistance to strengthen European Child Guarantee implementation including intersectoral government coordination SOPs, sustainable financing mechanisms, and an integrated data collection, monitoring, and reporting framework to guide evidence-based planning, accountability, and results across key sectoral systems (social welfare, education and health). The support should be based on concrete experience from EU member states already implementing the ECG framework. 

Policy Reform and Programme Design - Support the development of child poverty programmes, strategies and policies, including policy evidence and advocacy, and programme design. 


Financing for Scale- 

Identify opportunities for increased investment in poverty reduction and social sectors via fiscal space analysis and other tools

desc: Routine technical assistance is required to strengthen national policy and programme framework on the elimination of child labour while UNICEF supports the...
full: Routine technical assistance is required to strengthen national policy and programme framework on the elimination of child labour while UNICEF supports the Ministry of Labor and Social Security on development of a new national strategy and action plan on the elimination of child labour in Türkiye. 2026 will be the year to analyze the effectiveness of the previous programme and understanding the child labour prevalence in different sectors in the country.

desc: A national, government-led and costed plan to achieve universal birth registration, strengthening the CRVS system by removing administrative barriers...
full: A national, government-led and costed plan to achieve universal birth registration, strengthening the CRVS system by removing administrative barriers, standardizing procedures across governorates, and establishing a coordinated multisectoral framework. The initiative aims to reduce undocumented children and ensure sustainable, equitable access to birth registration for IDPs, returnees, children with disabilities, and other conflict-affected populations.

desc: Strengthen national responses to digital violence through policy reform, advocacy, and coordinated prevention systems
full: Provide technical support in the planning and organization of the international conference including the outreach of international speakers (alongside the SRSG VAC office); provide advocacy support to position Turkiye as a leader in addressing digital violence through mutli-stakeholder (GoT and the various line ministries/Min. Transportation and telecommunication, MoFSS, MOJ, MOI etc; Parliament, and private sector) engagement and accountability; provide advocacy, policy and legal support to ensure GOT and lawmakers use the child rights impact assessment as they are discussing the possible social media ban in the internet law; support children and youth led initiatives leading up to the international conference; support fundraising efforts

desc: Short-term technical assistance is required to support troubleshooting, configuration updates, workflow refinement, and quality assurance for CPIMS+/Primero...

desc: Review child protection policies, address coordination issues, maintain monitoring tools, and strengthen SBC materials and workforce training.
full:
1. Review and refine draft policies, protocols and implementation tools to ensure alignment with OECS, CARICOM and national child protection and MHPSS standards.
2. Provide troubleshooting and on-demand guidance to address coordination issues in emergency preparedness, response planning and multisector implementation.
3. Support routine data updates, monitoring reviews and maintenance of tracking tools to inform day-to-day programme adjustments and decision-making.
4. Offer targeted inputs on SBC materials, referral pathways and workforce training resources to strengthen ongoing delivery and community engagement.

desc: Operationalize Belize’s EVAC commitments through coordinated, multisector governance and service delivery at scale
full: LACRO support is required to strengthen the Country Office’s ability to guide Belize in operationalizing the EVAC Action Plan through an integrated, convergent approach across child protection, education, health and social protection. RO technical backstopping is needed on effective multisector coordination models, costing and sequencing of the Action Plan, and alignment with regional standards for EVAC prevention and response. LACRO’s expertise will help refine implementation tools, strengthen evidence-based intervention packages, and ensure cross-sector monitoring frameworks are fit for purposes. Additional RO support in capacity-building, South–South learning and resolving system bottlenecks will enable the CO to deliver sustained, high-quality technical assistance to national partners.

desc: Sustain digitised Risiha tools and government-managed ParentText/ParentApp while building in-house capacity for long-term ownership.
full:
1) Routine technical support is required throughout 2026 to sustain digitised Risiha tools and government-managed ParentText/ParentApp, while building in-house capacity for long-term ownership. This includes troubleshooting, refining data workflows, resolving IMS integration issues, and providing rapid inputs on content updates, analytics, quality assurance, and data protection.  Additional support is needed to develop a TOR for a UNICEF resource to oversee RapidPro and global digital parenting initiatives, defining governance roles, quality standards, and facilitating co-creation for scalable integration into government digital service delivery systems.
2) Birth registration estimates- South Africa 's statistics bureau is struggling to get a proper birth registration data- both through census and admin data. Few Academic Institutions (such as Children's Insitute ) has been trying to establish the model to estimate birth registration rate, but it has been a struggle. if UNICEF's data expert can provide in the methods of estimation, it can support the evidence generation and evidence-based policy level advocacy. 
3) Southern Africa migration coordination support- following the MIDSA in 2025, there has been Ministerial declaration adopted on Children on the Move, however, the practical coordination for case management between countries had been weak due to the lack of practical SOPs or focused coordination mechanism. It could be useful to have regional level coordination support between relevant countries, so there is a coherent SOP adopted across relevant SADC countries.

desc: Evaluate and renew national strategies to end child marriage through evidence-based, system-level reform
full:
Technical support for a multisectoral evaluation of the National Strategy to End Child Marriage (2017–2024), and in a second phase, support for the development of a new national strategy, elevating attention and action on a persisting issue requiring attention. (Policy Reform and Programme Design)
UNICEF Madagascar is requesting technical assistance to support the evaluation of the National Strategy on Ending Child Marriage (2017–2024). The implementation of the strategy faced a number of challenges, making a thorough evaluation essential before the development of a new strategy. During this period, the prevalence of girls aged 20–24 who were married before the age of 18 increased from 39% in 2018 to 42% in 2025, underscoring the need for sustained and strengthened national action. This includes continued multisectoral engagement and, based on the findings of the evaluation, the development of a new national strategy, with the mobilisation of key partners, including UNFPA.

3. Supporting the Rollout of the National Action Plan on Prevention and Response to Violence Against Children

This includes reviewing implementation guidelines and training materials, providing targeted inputs to strengthen coordination mechanisms, and troubleshooting operational challenges during rollout. desc: Transform juvenile justice systems to prioritize prevention, diversion, and rehabilitation through child-sensitive, rights-based approaches
full:
2.Juvenile Justice System Reform: Strengthening Prevention, Diversion, and Rehabilitation for Children in Conflict with the Law

This initiative aims to transform the juvenile justice system into a child-sensitive, rights-based framework that prioritizes prevention, diversion, and rehabilitation over punitive measures. The strategic purpose is to reduce the criminalization of children and ensure compliance with international standards, while addressing root causes of offending behavior. The intended system-level change is the institutionalization of integrated mechanisms—policy, protocols, and services—that enable early intervention, community-based alternatives, and structured rehabilitation programs. This reform will strengthen coordination among justice, social welfare, and education sectors, ensuring sustainable, scalable solutions that protect children’s rights and promote reintegration into society.
Technical assistance is required to re-design and institutionalize a child-sensitive juvenile justice framework that embeds prevention, diversion, and rehabilitation as core principles. This support will help develop national standards, operational guidelines, and inter-agency coordination mechanisms to replace punitive approaches with restorative practices. It will also guide the creation of structured diversion programs, community-based rehabilitation services, and capacity-building plans for justice and social welfare actors. By integrating these reforms into policy and practice, the assistance will deliver a sustainable system that protects children’s rights, reduces recidivism, and strengthens cross-sector collaboration for holistic child protection.

desc: Institutionalize technology-enabled prevention and early intervention within national child protection systems
full:
A system-strengthening initiative to institutionalize technology-enabled prevention and early intervention services within national child protection systems. This includes digitizing Risiha assessment and case management tools and transitioning ParentText/ParentApp to government ownership. Embedding digitized Risiha tools into government and organisational service delivery will enable timely identification of risks, consistent monitoring, and improved targeting of support for vulnerable children and families. Concurrently, establishing governance and technical capacity for ParentText/ParentApp will ensure sustainability and scale-up of evidence-based digital parenting support. Together, these actions will reinforce national delivery of family-strengthening interventions to prevent violence in homes and communities.
Technical assistance is required to design and implement the digitization of Risiha tools, including technical specifications, integration with provincial and national IMS, data standards, workflows, and quality assurance. Additional support is needed to guide the government-led pathway to ownership of ParentText/ParentApp, covering governance structures, content stewardship, costed implementation plans, data protection protocols, and long-term systems integration. This assistance will build national capacity to sustain and scale both platforms, ensuring they function as core components of child protection systems for prevention and early intervention.

desc: Scale justice for children from pilot initiatives to a national system
full:
Scaling up justice for children – from pilot to national model
UNICEF is currently implementing an integrated service package for justice for children in one major urban area (Kandy). By end-Q1 2026, the first six months of implementation of this pilot will provide operational evidence to assess functionality, gaps, and system-readiness for expansion. UNICEF will need to assess and use that evidence to propose a cost-efficient pathway for sustainable scale-up nationwide. This will likely include adjusting institutional responsibilities, refining the model based on early lessons, and developing a scalable financing and workforce approach. The specifics—appropriate sequencing, cost implications, and political feasibility—will become clearer once early performance data and feedback is analysed with UNICEF’s aim being to ensure that a realistic, evidence-based scale-up plan is on the table for government consideration by Q2/Q3 to feed into the preparation of the 2027 budget planning process.

desc: Climate finance and GCF technical assistance to support climate‑resilient WASH and water resources management
full: This technical assistance will provide specialized support to develop climate‑resilient WASH investment concepts and proposals, with a focus on Green Climate Fund (GCF) and other climate finance mechanisms. Support will include preparation of concept notes and full proposals; articulation of climate rationale, adaptation pathways, theories of change, budgets, and co‑financing strategies; and alignment with national climate priorities. The TA will integrate climate‑sensitive water resources management considerations and support compliance with climate finance requirements, including environmental and social safeguards.

desc: Technical assistance to integrate climate‑sensitive water resources management into WASH programming
full:
This technical assistance will support UNICEF Yemen to strengthen the integration of climate‑sensitive water resources management (WRM) into WASH programme design, planning, and investment decisions. The TA will focus on improving the sustainability and resilience of groundwater‑dependent water supply systems by incorporating water availability assessments, climate risk screening of water sources, sustainable abstraction principles, and basic groundwater protection measures into WASH programming.
The assistance will help embed WRM considerations into technical designs, programme strategies, and planning tools, ensuring that WASH investments are aligned with long‑term water resource availability and climate resilience objectives. The TA will also provide technical guidance to support informed decision‑making in severely water‑scarce and climate‑stressed contexts, contributing to reduced risks of over‑abstraction, system failure, and unintended negative impacts on water resources.

Expected Outputs / Deliverables:
Practical guidance for integrating WRM into WASH programme design
Climate‑risk screening inputs for water supply interventions
Strengthened alignment between WASH investments and water resource sustainability
Enhanced technical capacity of staff and partners on WRM‑WASH linkages

desc: Technical assistance on solarization and sustainability of water and sanitation systems under Yemen CPD 2027–2029
full:
This technical assistance will provide specialized support to strengthen the design, sustainability, and climate resilience of water and sanitation systems through appropriate solarization and energy‑efficient technologies. The TA will focus on reducing reliance on fuel‑based systems, improving service continuity, and strengthening operation and maintenance (O&M) models for water supply and sanitation infrastructure in fragile and resource‑constrained contexts.
Support will include technical review and guidance on solar‑powered water supply systems, energy needs assessment, system sizing, lifecycle costing, and performance standards. In addition, the TA will provide technical inputs to improve the sustainability of sanitation systems, including wastewater management and fecal sludge treatment facilities, where energy requirements affect functionality and long‑term operation. The assistance will ensure that solarization contributes to climate mitigation and adaptation objectives while improving technical quality, cost‑effectiveness, and durability of WASH services.

Expected Outputs / Deliverables:

Technical guidance on solar‑powered water supply systems
Improved design and sustainability of energy‑dependent sanitation infrastructure
Lifecycle costing and O&M recommendations for solarized WASH systems
Strengthened technical capacity of staff and partners on solarization and sustainability

desc: Technical assistance to strengthen sanitation and fecal sludge management (FSM) system
full:
This technical assistance will provide specialized support to strengthen sanitation and fecal sludge management (FSM) systems in humanitarian, early recovery, and development contexts. The TA will focus on system‑based sanitation approaches, moving beyond isolated infrastructure to improved service chains from containment, emptying, transport, treatment, and safe disposal or reuse.
Support will include technical guidance on context‑appropriate FSM models for urban and small‑town settings, integration of sanitation into cholera prevention and epidemic preparedness strategies, and development of sustainable operation and maintenance arrangements. The TA will also address climate and environmental considerations, including flood‑resilient sanitation, safe sludge treatment, and reduction of environmental contamination, thereby strengthening public health and environmental outcomes.

Expected Outputs / Deliverables
Technical guidance on sanitation and FSM service‑chain design
Context‑appropriate sanitation and FSM models for fragile settings
Strengthened integration of sanitation into cholera prevention programming
Improved technical capacity of staff and partners on sanitation systems

desc: Emergency‑to‑Systems (Humanitarian–Development Nexus WASH)
full:
This technical assistance will provide specialized support to strengthen the transition of WASH programming in Yemen from short‑term emergency response to sustainable, systems‑based service delivery. The TA will focus on integrating humanitarian, development, and climate‑resilience approaches to reduce dependency on recurrent emergency interventions while maintaining life‑saving capacity.
Support will include technical guidance on designing WASH interventions that bridge immediate response and longer‑term sustainability, including service delivery models, operation and maintenance arrangements, institutional roles, and financing considerations suitable for fragile and conflict‑affected contexts. The assistance will also help align emergency WASH programming with CPD priorities on climate‑resilient systems, sustainability, and national capacity strengthening.
The technical assistance will also contribute to WASH‑for‑Peace efforts, particularly in Taiz, by strengthening systems‑based WASH approaches that reduce competition over scarce resources, support equitable service delivery, and reinforce confidence‑building between communities and local service providers. By promoting sustainable, shared, and well‑managed WASH services, the emergency‑to‑systems transition will support social cohesion objectives and help mitigate local tensions linked to water scarcity and service access.

Expected Outputs / Deliverables

Technical guidance on emergency‑to‑systems WASH transition models
Practical recommendations for nexus‑oriented WASH programme design
Improved alignment between humanitarian response and CPD priorities
Strengthened internal understanding of systems‑based WASH in fragile contexts

desc: Technical assistance to strengthen climate‑resilient and standards‑compliant WASH in institutions, with a focus on World Bank‑aligned WASH in health facilities
full:
This technical assistance will provide specialized support to strengthen the technical quality, climate resilience, and sustainability of WASH services in institutions, with a primary focus on health facilities and targeted support to schools and learning environments where relevant. The TA will emphasize system functionality, reliability, and long‑term operation and maintenance, rather than standalone infrastructure delivery.
For health facilities, the assistance will focus on improving water supply reliability, sanitation and wastewater management, hygiene infrastructure, and waste management systems, ensuring alignment with international infection prevention and control (IPC) standards and World Bank Environmental and Social Standards (ESS). Particular attention will be given to climate‑resilient design, including water source resilience, backup systems, and adaptation to climate risks such as droughts and floods.
The TA will support technical design review, development of practical guidance and standards, and strengthening of operation and maintenance (O&M) arrangements for institutional WASH. This support will help reduce implementation and compliance risks, particularly in the context of World Bank‑financed health sector operations, while improving sustainability and service continuity in fragile and climate‑stressed environments.

Expected Outputs / Deliverables

Technical guidance and review inputs for WASH in health facilities and schools
Climate‑resilient design and O&M recommendations for institutional WASH systems
Strengthened alignment with IPC standards and World Bank ESS requirements
Improved technical capacity of staff and partners on institutional WASH quality assurance

desc: WASH Information Management, GIS & Digital Monitoring Systems
full:
This technical assistance will support UNICEF Yemen to strengthen WASH information management systems, including the use of GIS, digital monitoring tools, and integrated data platforms to support evidence‑based planning, programme monitoring, and decision‑making. The TA will focus on improving data quality, interoperability, and practical use of WASH data across humanitarian and development programming.
Support will include technical guidance on GIS‑based mapping of WASH infrastructure and services, development or refinement of digital monitoring frameworks, and integration of WASH data with climate risk, water resources, and health indicators where feasible. The assistance will also help strengthen capacity for routine data analysis, visualization, and use at national and sub‑national levels, ensuring that digital tools support programme quality, accountability, and adaptive management rather than parallel reporting systems.

Expected Outputs / Deliverables

GIS and digital monitoring guidance adapted to Yemen WASH context
Improved WASH data frameworks and visualization tools
Strengthened capacity of staff and partners on WASH information management
Better integration of WASH data into planning and reporting processes

desc: Strengthen Rural Community‑Based WASH Management & Sustainability Models
full:
This technical assistance will support UNICEF Yemen to strengthen community‑based WASH management models in rural areas, with a focus on sustainability, local ownership, and long‑term functionality of water and sanitation services. The TA will aim to move beyond short‑term community engagement toward structured, accountable, and technically sound management arrangements adapted to fragile and conflict‑affected contexts.
Support will include technical guidance on governance and management models for rural water schemes, roles and responsibilities of community institutions, basic financial management and cost recovery options, operation and maintenance arrangements, and linkages with local authorities and service providers. The assistance will also integrate climate resilience and water resource sustainability considerations into community‑based management approaches.

Expected Outputs / Deliverables

Practical guidance on community‑based WASH management models
Strengthened O&M and governance frameworks for rural WASH systems
Integration of climate resilience into rural WASH management approaches
Improved capacity of staff, partners, and communities

desc: Build a bankable investment pipeline to scale ECD and human capital reforms through IFI partnerships in Ecuador
full: The technical assistance aims to support El Salvador in conducting a comprehensive costing exercise for the implementation of the Early Childhood Development (ECD) National Policy for 2026–2030. This support will provide detailed financial analysis to estimate resource needs, identify funding gaps, and inform efficient allocation of public and donor resources. By applying standardized costing methodologies, facilitating inter-institutional coordination, and integrating sector-specific data, the assistance will help government counterparts develop a realistic, evidence-based budget framework. Ultimately, this will guide sustainable financing, enhance planning and accountability, and ensure that investments in ECD translate into tangible improvements in children’s development outcomes nationwide.

Assistance is also requested to ensure integration across sectors, promote results-based financing approaches where feasible, and strengthen UNICEF’s positioning for strategic dialogue and negotiations with IFIs, including support to define implementation pathways and sustainability strategies.

desc: Build governance and capacity for accountable public and private financing that delivers sustainable child outcomes
full:
1.  Policy Frameworks: TA support for development of strategy to  promote financing solutions and encourage private sector investment.
2. Capacity Building:
Capacity building of UNICEF staff and partners (Government agencies and financial institutions) to understand, assess, and support CO leadership and Partnerships for Innovative finance approaches for child outcomes. This includes: structured face to face and online workshops and trainings including toolkits  on public finance, and other financing, climate/social impact finance models, from a programmatic perspective. 
3. Governance and Risk Management
Technical advice on accountability frameworks and risk mitigation for public financing mechanism

desc: Continuous support to strengthen the National Agency for Social Protection’s (NASP) institutional capacity to apply RBM and PBB to social protection programmes
full: Continuous support to strengthen the National Agency for Social Protection’s (NASP) institutional capacity to apply RBM and PBB to social protection programmes. CoEs will help design RBM/PBB business processes and SoPs, accompany piloting in selected units, and build an internal cadre capable of sustaining training and coaching. Support will help NASP systematically link planning, budgeting and performance metrics, improving transparency, efficiency and allocation of resources toward child-centred priorities. RBM/PBB application will be further elaborated into the use of performance indicators at a service provider’s level, which will be facilitated by the application of outcome-based financing to engage state and non-state service providers. CoE will provide methodological leadership in designing appropriate procedures for the use of the performance indicators at the service provider’s level and applying the outcome-based financing.

desc: Reform Sudan’s social protection system into a child-sensitive, shock-responsive, government-led platform

desc: Implementation of UNICEF-led cash programmes (MCCT+ and emergency cash)
full: Ongoing TA is required to support implementation of UNICEF-led cash programmes (MCCT+ and emergency cash), including troubleshooting payment systems, refining MIS tools, improving linkage with PHC/nutrition services, analyzing routine data, refining SBC materials, and supporting grievance redress systems and understanding financial inclusion and literacy. Additional TA is needed for public finance for children, including budget tracking, expenditure reviews, routine analysis of federal and state allocations (health, education, WASH), and strengthening transparency tools. This day-to-day support ensures continuity of life-saving assistance while enabling evidence generation and capacity-building essential for the country’s systems transition

desc: Advance innovative financing for children by linking new mechanisms with national and subnational public finance in Indonesia

Support will focus on generating and translating evidence on child deprivations and financing gaps into actionable policy options, strengthening alignment with budget processes and fiscal frameworks.

desc: Under the social protection output, the care and support for children initiative is a relatively new programmatic area for the country programme, aligned...
full: Under the social protection output, the care and support for children initiative is a relatively new programmatic area for the country programme, aligned with the government’s broader priority on the care economy. To sustain implementation, targeted short-term and periodic technical support will be provided, including hands-on assistance to develop and operationalise internal office strategies, theories of change, and workplans; on-demand troubleshooting and technical review of concept notes, proposals, and guidance materials; and routine analytical and data support for programme decision-making. Support will also strengthen staff capacity through practical learning sessions, coaching on child-focused care systems and financing approaches, and strengthened private sector engagement.

desc: Peer support / discussion on the following issues: cash in emergency (war/conflict)
full:
Peer support / discussion on the following issues: cash in emergency (war/conflict); linkages between emergency cash ops and national SP system; poverty analysis; transparency and public participation in the national budget
Child Protection: The programme requires periodic technical support to inform design and sustain implementation. 
For Child Protection sector coordination, this includes validating survey results on localization and supporting the development of a related action plan, as well as supporting the drafting of the annual report. For social workforce strengthening and children's care reform, support is needed to participate in consultations, organize study tours, and review documents. On the integration of child protection and social policy, inputs are required to build the case management system for Cash++. For MRM/CAAC, short-term assistance is needed to organize and deliver a training-of-trainers on the MRM. Further technical support might be required to strengthen responses for children on the move. This would include guidance for the development of standardized cross-border case management mechanisms, ensuring coherent procedures.

desc: Strengthen UNICEF’s technical expertise and capacity on Public Finance Management (PFM), innovative financing, domestic financing and resource leveraging
full: The official shutdown of USAID as the largest donor supporting PFM has brought significant challenges to Nepal. UNICEF has an opportunity to step in and support the country’s technical expertise and capacity in PFM. Accordingly, technical assistance is required to map out the needs across different sectors, develop strategies, and strengthen relevant capacity of UNICEF staff and government. In addition, a robust strategy is required for strengthening innovative financing and sustainable domestic financing in low-middle income country context, leveraging Nepal's demonstrated co-financing model in various social sectors.
