desc: Establish an inclusive, shock-responsive social protection system to reduce child poverty and exclusion
full:
Design and Implementation of a Social Protection System for Children with Disabilities that would include the following:
1. Program Design and Targeting
• Inclusive Benefit Design: TA on disability-sensitive cash transfers, social insurance, and in-kind support.
• Eligibility Criteria: Technical input on functional assessments vs. medical models.
2. Social registry system: Creation of an interoperable social registry system.
3.  Accessibility and Service Delivery
• Universal Design Principles: Guidance on making registration and payment systems accessible.
• Assistive Technology Integration: Recommendations for linking social protection with assistive devices.
• Capacity Building: Training of the Government and CSO partners on disability inclusion.
4. Financial and Sustainability Analysis
• Costing Models: Technical support for fiscal space analysis and cost projections.
• Financing Strategies: TA support on leveraging domestic resources and donor funding.

full: Technical support in defining an agreed methodology with the National Statistics Office, support for development of related TORs and their implementation.  Support to develop and review draft reports and assist with the identification of policy recommendations.

full: Provide technical assistance for developing a roadmap for National Statistical Office establishes a website for Local Governments to key administrative data, key MICS indicators (through Small Area estimations) and budgetary information for their annual planning and budgeting cycle (Following the experience of UNICEF Uganda and Tanzania). Including identification of key data requirement for local governments planning, approaches for representative and disaggregated data, MoU for data exchange, automatization of regular data updates, etc.

desc: The F4C Roadmap developed by the office is ambitious and will require sustained technical support from the Centres of Excellence (CoEs)
full: The F4C Roadmap developed by the office is ambitious and will require sustained technical support from the Centres of Excellence (CoEs), particularly in the areas of costing and financing strategies, which are cross-cutting requirements for several sectors. In parallel, the CoEs’ support will be instrumental in advancing the office’s engagement with the Ministry of Finance on child budget tagging and with MoPEDIC on potential collaboration under the Integrated National Financing Framework (INFF) for social sector financing. Support to MoF in developing a child budget tagging methodology, integrate it withing the MIS systems and develop a model to track child spending based on the various macro-economic assumptions. Support SPCRM in its technical assitance to the respective ministries/councils leading on the costing of the National ECD strategy, National Girls Investment Framework and the National Social Protection Framework

desc: Reform and strengthen the national social protection system to reduce poverty and improve programme effectiveness
full:
UNICEF and ILO led a feasibility study for the implementation of a Universal child grant in 2023, which was validated by a Steering Committee in 2024.

The technical need is to support the rollout of the child grant program, including advocacy for the final ministerial endorsement and design of a roadmap for progressive implementation, including financing options and linkage with the social registry which is under development. This would build on the existing element of the social protection system aiming at strengthening it and increasing its child sensitivity.

desc: We request technical support to develop a new Country Programme Document (CPD) with a strong social policy focus
full:
We request technical support to develop a new Country Programme Document (CPD) with a strong social policy focus. The support is needed to help shape the overall policy framework and ensure the CPD is grounded in evidence-based social policy analysis.

In particular, we require technical assistance to design a clear and actionable strategy to strengthen public financing for children. This should include analysis of current financing gaps, policy and budgetary constraints, and options to improve the adequacy, efficiency, and equity of public spending for children.

The technical support will contribute to aligning the CPD with national priorities, strengthening its policy coherence, and ensuring that proposed interventions are feasible, sustainable, and impactful.

full: Promote internal advocacy for the scale up and mainstreaming of CBA modalities across sectors, including for incentive payments. Fundraising for increasing capacity to allow UNICEF to be fit for purpose to deliver CBA at scale in a risk informed manner.

full: Technical assistance will be requested to support the design and operationalization of child-focused financing strategies for poverty reduction and social protection, combining strengthened public financial management with innovative financing instruments such as debt swaps, development or social bonds, and results-based finance mechanisms. The assistance provided will support the development of concrete investment cases and financing frameworks for engagement with IFIs, especially CAF, targeting priority policy areas including early childhood development, education, health and nutrition, social protection, and climate-related vulnerabilities.

Technical assistance will focus on establishing linkages between emergency responses—specifically for populations displaced from the DRC and returnees from Tanzania with socio-economic support like cash transfer, village saving and loan group.

desc: Although the Merankabandi project successfully identified the full target population, budgetary constraints restricted coverage to only 50%
full:
Although the Merankabandi project successfully identified the full target population, budgetary constraints restricted coverage to only 50%. To close this coverage gap, UNICEF proposes a horizontal scale-up to extend support to the remaining 13,371 eligible vulnerable households.
Technical assistance is required to help fundraising for the expansion of Merankabandi Programme.

desc: UNICEF, WFP and World Bank support Social Registry)
full: UNICEF, WFP and World Bank support Social Registry). Following feasibility studies and roadmap updates, UNICEF facilitated establishment of the Working Group in 2024. In 2025, joint technical assistance is driving implementation, and the work bank funded the firm to build the system though the Steering Committee's formalization remains pending due to government restructuring. The support is to continue supporting this process.

desc: Provide technical assistance to support the Integrated Social Information System - SIIS, recently launched by the national government
full:
Provide technical assistance to support the Integrated Social Information System - 
SIIS, recently launched by the national government. Assistance will focus on strengthening the design of the information system and improving data processing (standardization and harmonization of administrative records, linking of data sources, etc.). This support will enable the national government to improve the targeting and the efficiency of social protection programs, the integration of diverse cash transfer policies, and the interoperability of administrative information.

desc: Support is required to align Communal Development Plans with results-based budgeting frameworks
full: Support is required to align Communal Development Plans with results-based budgeting frameworks. This assistance will ensure local planning is gender-responsive and child-sensitive, thereby addressing the needs of vulnerable groups while reinforcing social accountability and participatory governance.

desc: Mobilize private, climate, and IFI financing to scale multi-sectoral development programmes in Tanzania
full: Technical assistance to design a comprehensive package of multi sectoral innovative financing mechanism, including proposal development for global partnerships (GPE, GAVI, EAA), instruments and capacity development, including the Child Nutrition Fund Matching Windows application process and technical assistance for the Nutrition Promotion Levy, proposal development for climate and carbon financing, solar energy with special support on private sector engagements and banks.

desc: Expand El Salvador’s fiscal space through innovative financing to sustainably fund and scale child-focused social programmes.
full: The technical assistance aims to advance UNICEF’s engagement on innovative financing in El Salvador, with a particular focus on exploring debt-swap mechanisms that can expand fiscal space for priority social programmes benefiting children and adolescents. The support will strengthen ongoing discussions with CAF and national authorities, assess feasible financing structures, and help design mechanisms to direct debt savings toward sustainable, equity-focused social investments. By providing analytical guidance, partnership facilitation, and strategic planning support, this technical assistance seeks to translate innovative finance opportunities into concrete, country-level solutions that enhance the impact and sustainability of social programmes in El Salvador.

desc: Periodic budget analysis and targeted inputs for Public Finance for Children engagement, including review of national budget proposals and allocations.
full:
4. Budget Analysis and Public Finance for Children: Influencing National Resource Allocation in a High-Debt Context

Periodic technical support is required to conduct periodic budget analysis and provide targeted inputs for Public Finance for Children (P4FC) engagement. This includes reviewing national budget proposals, identifying allocations for child-focused sectors, and preparing briefs to influence resource mobilization and protection in a high-debt context. Assistance will also involve troubleshooting fiscal data gaps, validating expenditure tracking tools, and supporting advocacy materials for dialogue with the Ministry of Finance and Parliament. These practical, day-to-day interventions will enable UNICEF to sustain engagement in the budget cycle and ensure children’s priorities remain protected in national resource allocation.

desc: Institutionalize annual measurement of public investment in early childhood to guide sustainable ECD financing in Paraguay
full: The CO requires specialized technical assistance in PF4C to estimate public investment in early childhood, identify fiscal spaces within the national expenditure budget, and model the returns on investment and the costs of not investing. This assistance will be used to advocate for policies, budgets, and the prioritization of early childhood at the national level.

The assistance will strengthen operational capacity to manage fiscal transfers, improve resource allocation, and ensure predictable and equitable financing for children’s programmes. It will include practical guidance, technical review, and capacity support for government institutions and local authorities, enhancing service delivery and programmatic outcomes, and identifying opportunities for domestic resource mobilization and partnerships to sustain investments in children.

desc: MCO has been successful in mobilizing resources to expand Zara Mira social protection programme beyond the CPD ceiling
full: MCO has been successful in mobilizing resources to expand Zara Mira social protection programme beyond  the CPD ceiling. However, all current donor funding will expire by 2027 and the office needs to be strategic to attract more funding for social protection. This involves a thorough to reshape the programme in light of the new international funding landscape as well as a reality check. What has the programme been able to achieve so far (the endline impact evaluation is planned in 2027, but the PDM reports and the capitalization study can already point out to programme takeaways)? How can we package the programme to sell it to potential and interested donors? Do we actually need to expand the programme? These are big areas of reflexion for which we need CoE support in 2027.

Assistance is also needed to build capacity for transparent, data-driven decision-making and results-based budgeting across key sectors for children

desc: Build a national innovative finance ecosystem to mobilize and align public and private capital for children in Colombia
full: Technical support will focus on strategic engagement with multilateral development banks, particularly CAF and IDB/BID, leveraging a regional perspective to position innovative finance for children, hands-on technical assistance to assess the feasibility of parametric insurance for child-centered social services, and practical support to adapt UNICEF legal, financial, and partnership tools to enable innovative financing modalities. Working closely with relevant *Centers of Excellence (CoE) will help ensure coherence, tool development, and systematic knowledge sharing across the organization, and allow us to learn from other countries.

desc: Overall, support is valued in reviewing documents and proposals, routine data support, and targeted inputs to address specific needs
full: Overall, support is valued in reviewing documents and proposals, routine data support, and targeted inputs to address specific needs. In particular, support is requested to help define a financing framework for the National Policy on Children and Adolescents and to support the implementation of the Public Finance for Children (PF4C) framework in Chile through the introduction of a child-focused budget marker.

desc: Strengthen social policy and public finance systems to scale and sustain child-focused interventions nationally and locally
full: Technical assistance to review the present Public Finance/budgeting practices, identify the bottlenecks, and suggest the strategy to engage/influence the PF/budgeting processes/mechanisms to deliver better results for children at decentralized levels.

desc: Periodic practical support is required to sustain and advance ongoing programme implementation
full: Periodic practical support is required to sustain and advance ongoing programme implementation, with a particular focus on climate-responsive social protection, as well as leveraging and financing for children interventions.

desc: Strengthen public financial management to scale domestic investment in human capital and essential services in South Sudan
full: Technical assistance is required to support government counterparts in strengthening core PFM functions relevant to essential service delivery, including budgeting, execution, financial reporting, internal controls, and expenditure tracking at national and sub-national levels. Advocacy both at legislative and administrative levels. Support will also focus on aligning sector financing modalities (including donor-supported programmes) with government systems and building institutional capacity for sustainability and accountability.

desc: Support the implementation of the local governance model, taking into account the substantive differences in the territories of the most vulnerable children...
full: Support the implementation of the local governance model, taking into account the substantive differences in the territories of the most vulnerable children and adolescents.

desc: Design and scale a child-sensitive, equity-focused social protection system anchored in public finance reform
full: Technical assistance to support the Government in designing, reforming, and scaling a child-sensitive and equity-focused social protection system, anchored in public finance reform and aligned with national priorities. The support promotes a UN-wide, UNICEF-led shift from fragmented, programme-based interventions to a coherent, government-financed system that systematically reaches children and vulnerable households. It focuses on strengthening policy design, governance, and financing, integrating child poverty evidence, equity and disability inclusion. The assistance also strengthens Public Finance for Children and the INFF, and supports the transition to results-based, investment-oriented planning through modelling, costing, and fiscal analysis, translating evidence into policy dialogue, investment cases, and strategic advocacy.

desc: Expand coverage and adequacy of government-led child benefits to reach children in multidimensional poverty in Vietnam
full: Technical assistance to design a child-sensitive social protection system, including a child benefits scheme with a long-term view to achieving universal coverage for all children.

desc: Developing a Child-sensitive Integrated National Financing Framework (INFF) (Social Policy & Multisectoral)
full:
Developing a Child-sensitive Integrated National Financing Framework (INFF) (Social Policy & Multisectoral).
Technical assistance provided to support UNICEF contribution to the joint UN Integrated National Financing Framework (INFF) initiative (launch expected in 2026), especially on the Development Finance Assessment (DFA) component, with a focus on climate change, nutrition, social protection and learning to earning transitions.

desc: Quality assurance of PF4C-related documents and analytical products
full: Quality assurance of PF4C-related documents and analytical products, including the child protection continuum and budget analysis, child-focused budget transparency initiatives, , National C-PEM, budget briefs, INFF related papers, and disability inclusion products.

desc: Standardize and scale cash transfer systems to improve quality, accountability, and alignment with national systems in South Sudan
full: Technical assistance is required to support implementation of HQ and RO Cash Learning Review recommendations through establishment of a strengthened direct delivery model. This includes support to set up a Direct Delivery Unit, review budget structure and affordability, define reporting lines, and realign existing office capacity. Assistance will strengthen risk mitigation measures in the absence of biometric authentication, enhance field monitoring of payments through CTG capacity (including de-duplication and grievance management), improve FSP performance and contract management, operationalize HOPE through training and prioritised use, adjust accountability frameworks, and establish a Rep-chaired cash task force to strengthen governance, oversight, and donor engagement.

desc: Periodic practical support is required to maintain momentum in the ongoing youth policy reform and programme design processes
full:
Periodic practical support is required to maintain momentum in the ongoing youth policy reform and programme design processes. 
Support with technical expertise to assess the testing phase of uSupport to capture lessons learnt and define areas of improvement having in mind scale up and sustainability of the uSupport platform. This includes also discussing long-term sustainability, and explore potential new features, including AI integration.

desc: A four-day training on cash-based assistance in emergencies will be facilitated by COE Economic and Social Policy
full: A four-day training on cash-based assistance in emergencies will be facilitated by COE Economic and Social Policy. The training supports the introduction, scale-up, and institutionalization of cash-based assistance through national systems for emergency response. It targets mid- to senior-level officials from key ministries, including social protection, disaster risk management, finance, and local government, as well as relevant parastatal bodies at national and sub-national levels. The workshop will build a shared understanding of how and why to operationalize cash assistance, identify system gaps and opportunities, clarify partner contributions, and define priority next steps, laying a foundation for collaboration and faster disaster responses

desc: Integrate Public Financing for children (PF4C) to build internal capacity and enhance impact at scale.
full: Technical assistance is needed to institutionalize PF4C as a core programming strategy within UNICEF Pakistan, enabling all sectors to influence public finance for children. A key focus will be on strengthening internal staff capacity to understand and apply PF4C tools—including budget tagging, expenditure analysis, and fiscal space reviews—across planning, budgeting, and advocacy. Provide guidance for the development of a PF4C integration roadmap, training packages, and hands-on tools. TA will also enhance UNICEF’s engagement with government during the 2026–27 budget cycle to improve child-focused allocations, budget execution, and sector financing outcomes.

desc: Support on study of fiscal space for social protection
full: Capacity building on financing for social protection and on social assistance to new state department of children (national) and key sub-national governments on child-sensitive social protection

Technical assistance to strengthen UNICEF and national partners’ capacity to systematically integrate disability-sensitive approaches into PF4C, public policy dialogue, and programme design, through the development and use of disability-focused knowledge products, policy notes, and programme-oriented analytical tools.

desc: Advance a debt swap mechanism to sustainably finance nutrition and immunization through strengthened fiscal, legal, and negotiation capacity.
full: UNICEF Angola requires specialized technical assistance to advance the Debt Swap for Nutrition and Immunization Supplies initiative led by the Ministry of Finance. Support is needed to strengthen analytical, legal, and financial structuring capacities to engage effectively with DFC, commercial banks, and regional financing institutions. TA will help refine the debt swap mechanism, model fiscal impacts, coordinate multi stakeholder negotiations, and ensure alignment with national nutrition and immunization priorities. It will also reinforce the Social Policy section’s role in providing high quality economic, programmatic, and negotiation support to Government counterparts.

desc: TA support for the public finance management deliverables
full: TA support for the public finance management deliverables, such as Budget Briefs, Execution Reports and the Investment Case for Universal Child Benefit and the Disability Grant with focus on improving advocacy for the child-sensitive budgets and greater impact in the priorities for children.

desc: Develop a plan to scale a child benefit as part of the social safety net nationally
full: Develop a plan to scale a child benefit as part of the social safety net nationally. This will involve costing, planning, modeling and exchanges with other offices (Oman in early 2026). We will continue to draw on the regional contract with Cornerstone to support costing of the social service workforce required for a child benefit to deliver at scale. As we seek to enhance the job descriptions and training of social workers in Iraq, to better provide case management, we would like to draw on examples from other countries, particularly any that have been effective with children with disability. Lastly, we will continue to depend on the work the RO was doing to support disability inclusion training (funded by Saudi Agfund), the next phase of which will focus on early identification. Similarly, we could use support to promote the Amman-Berlin declaration with GoI and Germany embassy partners/use of the newly created Joint Help Desk.

desc: Establish an integrated subnational system linking child data, planning, and results-based budgeting to guide equitable public investment.
full: Sustained technical assistance is required to design and operationalize an integrated sub-national system that links child-focused monitoring data (administrative, survey, and geospatial), participatory planning, programme prioritization, and results-based budgeting. Support will harmonize sectoral data flows, strengthen local government planning and PFM capacities, embed equity and child-rights lenses in budget processes, and align local plans with national policy frameworks to enable evidence-based allocation of public resources for children at scale.

CoE support is essential to provide global expertise on child benefits, adaptive social protection, and IFI engagement, ensuring scalability and sustainability in a low-income, fragile context.

desc: Secure sustainable domestic and innovative financing to protect equitable child-focused services during Côte d’Ivoire’s income transition.
full: Technical assistance to conduct child-focused expenditure analysis across key social sectors and design sustainable domestic financing strategies. Support will include building government and civil society capacity for child budgeting and expenditure tracking from national to local levels. TA will also explore fiscal space and taxation reforms—national and municipal—to integrate child priorities into resource mobilization strategies for the next decade. This will enable Côte d’Ivoire to institutionalize financing frameworks that safeguard investments in health, education, nutrition, social protection, child protection and early childhood development, ensuring resilience and continuity of essential services for children amid shifting economic conditions.

- TA on integrating social protection programs and benefits into national disaster risk reduction strategies, especially early warning systems, with a focus on building anticipatory response mechanisms that trigger timely support for children and families before shocks, including by peer learning and exchange of experiences with other countries.
- TA on the design and implementation of social protection programs and benefits for indigenous families, with a focus on ensuring inclusivity, including by exchanging experiences with countries such as Peru, Guatemala, and Bolivia.

desc: Transition from humanitarian response to development ensuring resilience in each sector of country program.
full:
As the country transitions from the immediate response to Hurricane Melissa toward sustainable and resilient development, technical assistance is required to support this shift. Priority needs include expertise in climate-resilient education infrastructure, shock-responsive social protection, multisectoral coordination, and the integration of disaster risk reduction and mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) into national systems.

Technical support will be critical to guide the Government of Jamaica in translating emergency actions into durable, system-wide reforms that strengthen service delivery, protect vulnerable children and families, and build institutional resilience to future shocks.

Assist with drafting a pilot design plan, proposed benefit parameters, and a resource mobilization strategy to inform engagement with  DPMO, MOF, MOEPD and development partners. Support will include reviewing background documents, troubleshooting data gaps, and providing short technical notes to sustain ongoing programme progress and decision-making.

full: Provide routine technical assistance to support the Government (particularly  DPMO)  in initiating the development of a National Social Protection Act that will establish an overarching legal framework for social protection delivery in Eswatini. Required support includes legal review of existing policy instruments, drafting technical comments, preparing issue notes, and consolidating inputs from across sectors. Routine troubleshooting and quick technical checks will be needed to sustain progress as the drafting process may begin in  Q 2 2026.

This includes developing practical training materials, reviewing existing budget structures, drafting step-by-step case examples, and troubleshooting specific questions from MOF and line ministries. Support will also involve tailoring methodologies to Eswatini’s budget cycle and ensuring alignment with national planning and monitoring frameworks.

desc: Transition humanitarian programmes toward scalable, sustainable systems delivering child outcomes under the humanitarian–development nexus
full: UNICEF operates in a VUCA context in Venezuela. CO will require support to develop an evidence-generation strategy, given political sensitivity and data scarcity, to inform policy advocacy with national authorities. In the context of drastic funding cuts, the CO is seeking innovative and flexible financing opportunities, including private-sector engagement and IFI, to achieve results at scale. The CO is moving from a project-driven approach to a more systemic approach. In align with the ambition of UNICEF SP, the CO will need support in programme management for scale up.

This includes international benchmarking of child guarantee models, refinement and validation of policy options and financing mechanisms (including innovative instruments such as outcome-based financing and social bonds), and high-level advocacy and CoE engagement with senior decision-makers and the public to build consensus for adoption.

desc: Design a strategic, system-level initiative to reduce child multi-dimensional poverty nationwide through coordinated policy and programme action.
full: Technical assistance is required to support a conceptual shift from absolute poverty to multidimensional poverty among major state and non-state stakeholders; assist the government in developing a minimum package of social services that combines essential health, education, and social protection interventions to address key vulnerabilities and ensure equitable access nationwide; establish baseline and progress indicators and institutionalize measurement of multidimensional poverty; and undertake other relevant actions to strengthen policy coherence, monitoring systems, and evidence-based decision-making for comprehensive poverty reduction.

full: Support in design of adaptive resilient social protection CCTs models

full: Technical assistance to design a nationally scalable childcare model that addresses the unique needs of adolescent mothers and caregivers. This model will provide safe, affordable, and accessible childcare services to enable adolescent girls to remain in school and participate in economic activities, while ensuring quality standards and sustainability through integration with social protection and education systems. The support will include technical design, policy guidance, and capacity building, including an evaluation to generate evidence for scale-up.

This includes support for the design and implementation of the vulnerability and social welfare needs assessments; formulation of the adaptive social protection strategy; development of an approach for awareness-raising among officials and the public; and technical advice on social welfare service concepts, integration guidelines, standards and costing methodology, so that these outputs translate into a coherent reform and investment agenda.

desc: Strengthen public expenditure planning and financing systems to prioritize high-equity investments for children
full: CoE can provide sector-specific fiscal and expenditure expertise for nutrition, health, WASH, education and social protection — including costing essential service packages, subsidy impact modelling, and budget tagging. Support multi-year and innovative financing models tailored to each sector, such as pooled health funds, school rehabilitation financing, WASH infrastructure guarantees, and remittance-linked nutrition support. Advise on digital and energy-efficient systems, including interoperable registries, digital payments for services, supply-chain optimisation for medical and nutrition commodities, and efficient WASH and school infrastructure. It can strengthen sector data and accountability, enabling dashboards, predictive analytics and safeguards to ensure reforms protect vulnerable households and service continuity.

1 Technical assistance t is required to design and operationalize innovative financing options that mobilize resources for child-sensitive emergency preparedness and response, and scale up child grants, including for children on the move.
2. Technical assistance is required to conduct a high-level macroeconomic analysis in Trinidad and Tobago on the impact of multidimensional child poverty on the economy, human capital development, and national stability.

desc: Technical assistance required from CoE to design Syria’s National Livelihoods and Economic Inclusion Policy
full: Technical assistance required from CoE to design Syria’s National Livelihoods and Economic Inclusion Policy, ensuring alignment with social protection, youth employment, and economic recovery. This will lead to a government-endorsed national framework linked to Syrian National Social Protection Framework. Targeted guidance is needed to integrate livelihood pathways into Unified Social Registry by developing profiles and eligibility criteria for youth, women, displaced populations and persons with disabilities. This support enables the system to match households with employment pathways, advancing a scalable “cash-to-jobs” model. The expected result is a more inclusive labour market pathway for crisis-affected populations and reduced long-term dependency.

desc: Minimum package of services, UCB advocacy
full: (1) Supporting the conceptualization and development of minimum packages of services/cash and care packages. As noted, we are currently working with a team of national and international consultants on the conceptualization of a Minimum Package of Social Services. Once the concept is finalized (expected by late February), we would value an external professional review to critically assess the design, assumptions, and coherence of the proposed package. (2) Supporting the development of advocacy pitches for universal child benefits. We have made significant progress in advocating for the expansion of cash benefits for children, resulting in substantial increases in both value and coverage. However, these gains have so far been achieved within the Targeted Social Assistance programme. We would like to strengthen our advocacy for a universal (or quasi-universal) child benefit. While we have already conducted analytical work—including simulations on coverage, poverty impact, and fiscal costs—we need support in translating this evidence into a compelling advocacy narrative and strategy.

desc: Support the Government in designing and operationalizing a coherent Learning to Earning system that connects education, TVET, skills development, green
full: Technical assistance to support the Government in designing and operationalizing a coherent Learning to Earning system that connects education, TVET, skills development, green skills, entrepreneurship, and employability pathways for adolescents and youth. desc: UNICEF Armenia Country Office is keen to engage in the expansion of innovative learning programmes under UNICEF’s new global education strategy, particularly it
full:
UNICEF Armenia Country Office's interest in exploring the opportunity to participate in the expansion of innovative learning programmes, announced as part of UNICEF's transformative global education strategy launched at Educa in Helsinki last week. I am particularly inspired by the Strategy's deliberate shifts toward putting teachers and learners at the center while prioritizing equity.
 
The Government of Armenia recognizes the critical importance of EdTech to transform education delivery. Active discussions are currently underway at the Ministry of Education, Science, Culture and Sports (MoESCS) with relevant stakeholders regarding the role of EdTech and AI in education. While several pilot and innovative initiatives exist in both formal and informal education, a comprehensive framework and system-wide approach are currently missing—making this an opportune moment for UNICEF strategic intervention.
 
Armenia presents a unique opportunity for scaling innovative learning programmes due to the strong government commitment to digital transformation and presence of companies delivering innovative solutions. In 2024, the MoESCS adopted new state standards for Digital Literacy and Computer Science (DLCS), aiming to equip students with practical, future-ready programming skills, computational thinking, and digital literacy. To ensure effective implementation of these standards, UNICEF, in collaboration with the Ministry and in partnership with the Foundation for Armenian Science and Technology (FAST), is supporting a pilot programme to capacitate DLCS teachers to deliver the new curriculum. We are simultaneously supporting the digitalization of teacher training courses through the integration of LMS.
 
Participation in the global innovative learning programmes would enable Armenia to:
 

Build upon existing initiatives with a comprehensive, system-wide approach

Align national efforts with global best practices and evidence-based solutions

Accelerate progress toward measurable learning outcomes with equity at the core

Strengthen the capacity of teachers through innovative tools and methodologies
 
I would greatly appreciate the opportunity to discuss Armenia's potential participation in the initial phase or subsequent phases of the Strategy's rollout. A meeting would allow us to:
 

Explore specific programme alignment with Armenia's education priorities

Discuss implementation modalities and timelines

Identify partnership opportunities and resource requirements

Determine next steps for formal engagement

desc: Burundi faces foundational learning challenges due to inconsistent school readiness, weak early-grade pedagogy, and limited support for struggling learners.
full: Burundi faces foundational learning challenges due to inconsistent school readiness, weak early-grade pedagogy, and limited support for struggling learners. The transition from Kirundi to French complicates learning. Despite education being the most funded social sector, technical assistance is needed to (i) strengthen school readiness frameworks and teaching practices; (ii) integrate readiness and foundational learning indicators into monitoring systems, and (iii) develop a sustainable financing strategy to ensure equitable, efficient reforms aligned with national policies and every child's right to quality education.

full: Achieving universal preschool access in BiH remains a systemic challenge, especially considering fragmented governance, inadequate public funding, and insufficient private sector engagement. desc: Advance teacher development, leadership, and digital transformation in education to improve access and learning.
full: The Education system counts about 57,000 classrooms, more than half of which are built with non‑durable materials, making them unusable during adverse weather conditions. More than 3 million school‑age children are either out of school or at risk of dropping out of the education system. The Government has made a strategic bet on the use of ICTs to help close the education gap. This commitment is reflected in the establishment of a dedicated directorate in charge of ICTs within the education system. The National Development Plan (PND) further reinforces this strategic orientation as a tool to reduce the digital divide while improving access to quality education.

desc: Review and adapt national policies and strategies to integrate child-centered climate resilience across social sectors. Support will include designing actionabl
full: Technical assistance to review and adapt national policies and strategies to integrate child-centered climate resilience across social sectors. desc: The technical assistance aims to support El Salvador in strategically integrating artificial intelligence (AI) across the education system to enhance
full: The technical assistance aims to support El Salvador in strategically integrating artificial intelligence (AI) across the education system to enhance learning outcomes, equity, and teacher quality. The support includes developing data-driven decision-making platforms, adapting international best practices, and ensuring inclusive approaches for vulnerable populations. Ultimately, this technical assistance will help operationalize AI solutions, strengthen national capacity, and accelerate the country’s path toward globally competitive education outcomes and sustainable system-level transformation

full:
Analytical inputs and support with drafting a technical fiche for a EUR 6.5 mill system-reform programme to operationalize the reform agenda; costed policy options; implementation planning; and preparation of formal agreements between UNICEF and government counterparts.Additional information from Dep Rep:

Dear Mitsue,
 
Thank you for your follow up to our TA request, whichconcerns our ESF+ funded programme pipeline with the Ministry of Education, entitled “Interventions to strengthen school structures of the educational system to support the education of refugee students,”, expected to commence in Q3 2026. Within this programme UNICEF will provide to MOE assistance to support the development of a coherent, national-level set of education system frameworks, specifically:a nationwide education capacity-building framework for      educators and school leadership;a structured framework for education materials aimed      at supporting refugee and migrant students;a framework for parallel and afternoon educational      support, ensuring alignment with formal schooling; anda framework for strengthening institutional roles and      improving inter-ministerial coordination related to refugee education.
The Ministry has requested that we draft the technical fiche by early Q2, which will need to describe in detail the work packages, activities, implementation modalities, and budget structure. To provide this TA support, we estimate a one-week in-person mission by one COE expert, followed by three weeks of remote support at approximately six hours per day.
I am also copying here Despina to add any additional clarifications on the exact nature of the support requested as needed.
All the best,
Aspa

desc: Showcasing India's NIPUN Bharat initiative to improve foundational learning at a day-long pre-meeting, proceeding the Africa Foundational Learning Exchange (FLE
full: India’s NEP 2020 prioritizes a continuous early‑learning pathway and launched NIPUN Bharat to ensure all children achieve foundational literacy and numeracy by Grade 3. Despite COVID‑19 setbacks, national surveys show improved early‑grade reading and math, aided by evidence‑based reforms, structured pedagogy, multilingual materials, and strong institutional support. A one‑day learning exchange, proceeding the Africa Foundational Learning Exchange (FLEX) in Malawi, 15-17 July will bring together officials and experts to share India’s lessons with African counterparts on scaling foundational learning. This was discussed during Pia's mission to India.

desc: Documentation of the India's experience on AI-based early warning system for out-of-school children
full: Pia's mission to India brought up the country's experience on AI-based EWS on OOSC. She suggested that we document the experience, so that it could be shared widely. This responds well to the government/MOE's interest in sharing their experience widely and the UNICEF CO's commitment to supporting the government in doing so.

desc: Strengthening State Capacity for Psychometric Analysis and Comparable Learning Assessment Results

desc: Provide guidance on successful EdTech tools and programmes to improve foundational learning, with focus on the use of EdTech to assist teachers in conducting fo
full:
UNICEF Indonesia office has recently kicked off a partnership with Gates Foundation and Tanoto Foundation on a programme to improve foundational learning (FAASTER programme).



As part of this programme, we have an EdTech component where we plan to use an app to assist teachers in conducting formative assessment and using student learning data to better inform classroom instructions. The goal is to strengthen individualized/remedial support for low-performing students through effective use of learning data.



We are currently in the inception phase and consulting relevant stakeholders to inform our programme design. We would greatly appreciate the opportunity to connect with your team to learn about successful EdTech programmes in other UNICEF country contexts, key lessons learned, and considerations for implementation.


In particular, we are interested in EIDU’s work in Kenya, which supports teachers in using technology for lesson planning and assessment; our programme is also centered around structured pedagogy as is the EIDU platform. We would appreciate if we can also discuss potential ways to connect with EIDU, as I understand they are part of the Blue Unicorn portfolio

desc: UNICEF Iraq is transitioning from fragmented youth skilling initiatives to integrated, climate-responsive Learning-to-Earning pathways within national systems.
full: UNICEF Iraq is transitioning from fragmented youth skilling initiatives to integrated, climate-responsive Learning-to-Earning pathways within national systems. These pathways respond to youth unemployment while addressing climate risks and green economy opportunities. Technical assistance is required to institutionalize these models within MoE, MoLSA, MoYS, MOEN and relevant ministries, ensuring alignment with labour market needs, national climate commitments (NDCs/NAPs), and inclusive access for girls, displaced youth, and vulnerable host-community youth.Sustained technical assistance is required to support policy and systems reform for climate-responsive Learning-to-Earning pathways. This includes developing a national L2E framework integrating climate education, green skills, labour market information systems, and protection/MHPSS services; strengthening inter-ministerial coordination and institutional roles; embedding youth participation mechanisms; and supporting costing and financing strategies for results at scale - aligned with climate adaptation and mitigation priorities."

desc: Request to reinstate the Learning Passport (LP) platform in Mozambique, following its shutdown last November due to inactivity.
full:
UNICEF Mozambique has reassessed the situation and successfully re-engaged the Ministry of Education, renewing interest in leveraging technology to transform the education system. The urgency is clear: Mozambique faces significant challenges in ensuring learning continuity. Over two million children remain out of school, dropout rates are high and learning outcomes for those enrolled are alarmingly low. The education system is overstretched and underfunded, and the outlook is further complicated by rapid population growth, economic stagnation, ongoing conflict, and the impacts of climate change.

To reverse this downward trend, a bold transformation of the education sector is needed—one that harnesses the power of technology to expand access, improve quality, and build resilience. In close coordination with the Ministry of Education, UNICEF Mozambique is committed to reviving the Learning Passport with a clear and strategic vision and building on lessons learned from previous efforts:

• To deliver curriculum-aligned content to children both in and out of school
• To build foundational and 21st-century skills
• To strengthen teacher capacity through digital professional development modules

full: TA to develop national guidelines and a digital ecosystem for decodable readers to strengthen systematic literacy instruction in early grades by establishing clear standards for the creation, adaptation, and use of digital decodable readers across all approved 11 languages; ensuring accessibility and inclusive design; integrating mechanisms for evaluating pedagogical effectiveness; and regulating ethical distribution and learner data protection. The activity also includes supporting teachers through embedded guidance, modelling resources, and structured implementation support.

This has been an area identified as a priority for Nepal by the Global Partnership for Education and several key bilateral donors, which will potentially increase additional financing to support this initiative in 2026 and beyond.

desc: Over the previous five-year period, with UNICEF’s TA and strong partnerships with the MoE and World Bank, the new preschool
full: Over the previous five-year period, with UNICEF’s TA and strong partnerships with the MoE and World Bank, the new preschool curriculum ‘Years of Ascent’ was rolled out nationwide, marking a systemic shift towards playbased, childled learning. During the four-year period, 22,388 educators (95% of the total number) in public and private ECE institutions were empowered through a comprehensive capacity-building programme (blended training, mentoring, communities of practice) aimed at strengthening competencies for a new, high-quality, inclusive pedagogy, and more than 235,000 children benefited from their enhanced competencies. Peer learning and mentoring proved particularly effective, with evaluations confirming that around two thirds of preschool teachers remained open to change over a two-year period of curriculum implementation. CO is envisaging similar evidence-based teacher capacity building strategy.

desc: Supporting successful curriculum reform and strengthening learning outcomes
full: Sri Lanka begins rolling out its new curriculum in Grades 1 and 6 in 2026, alongside a national learning assessment. This reform has the potential to modernise teaching and restore learning trajectories after years of disruption, but challenges remain. One key challenge is not the design of the curriculum but its acceptance—by teachers accustomed to tuition-driven, exam-focused practices, and by communities that may be wary of pedagogical change. The priority for 2026 is to ensure the reform succeeds by helping identify bottlenecks early, monitoring classroom realities, and supporting strategies that build teacher and community confidence in the new approach. As details of implementation become clearer in Q1, UNICEF will refine the support package, with particular attention to child-centered pedagogy and development of a teacher professional development framework, strengthening monitoring and mentoring mechanisms, education behaviour change, communication, and practical tools that help teachers make the shift in pedagogy but support to ensure a successful reform journey for Sri Lanka is required.

desc: Develop GCF proposal for climate-resilient school models.

The fast-paced process targets presenting a GCF-approved concept note at the June Executive Board meeting in Tajikistan. TA will adapt UNICEF’s global best practices for climate-resilient, child-sensitive infrastructure through national green building standards and a sustainable financing mechanism, aiming to eventually scale the model to 4,000+ schools. These standards and mechanisms will guide government and partners, fostering harmonized climate-smart investments in schools, health facilities, and child-serving institutions, with impact beyond the GCF project.

full: Technical support for evidence-based school network optimisation and investment planning. This includes refining methodologies for school mapping, demographic and infrastructure analysis, climate-resilient design considerations and GBV risk mitigation, and prioritization tools for capital investments. CoEs will help build national and local capacities to apply these models, thereby promoting transparent and equitable planning, improving learning conditions, and enabling Government to strategically expand and modernize the school network.

desc: Operationalize national learning assessment frameworks.
full: Continuous support to fully operationalize the national Learning Assessment System Framework as a core component of Uzbekistan’s education reform agenda. CoEs will advise on governance structures, assessment standards, tools, data quality assurance and mechanisms to translate assessment findings into instructional improvement. Support will ensure coherence with FCDO and IsDB investments and build national capacities for routine use of learning assessment data to guide curriculum reform, resource allocation and teacher professional development.

desc: Develop FLN indicators and monitoring tools.

desc: Conduct feasibility study and design innovative ECEC financing.
full: Technical assistance to design and implement a comprehensive feasibility study and investment case for innovative ECEC financing. This will include sector diagnostics, costing and sustainability scenarios, and design of context-appropriate financing instruments (e.g. blended and results-based models). Support will also strengthen national capacity and advisory engagement with IFIs (IsDB, WB) so that Uzbekistan can design, negotiate and manage scalable, child-centred investments in early learning and childcare. costing and sustainability scenarios, and design of context-appropriate financing instruments (e.g. blended and results-based models). Support will also strengthen national capacity and advisory engagement with IFIs (IsDB, WB) so that Uzbekistan can design, negotiate and manage scalable, child-centred investments in early learning and childcare.

desc: Strengthen girls’ STEM, digital, and green-skills pathways.
full: Support learning-to-earning pathways - fostering girls' skills in STEM and digital, green skills/LIFE SKILLS/TVET/Out of School vocational training interventions and enhancing girls access to role models, mentorship including through strengthening private sector partnerships for gender equality outcomes.

desc: Model inclusive education systems at school and community level.
full: Support to advance Uzbekistan’s inclusive education (IE) agenda through practical modelling of the national IE strategy at school and community levels. CoEs will advise on development of implementation guidelines, service and referral pathways, teacher tools, UDL-aligned pedagogical approaches and mechanisms for community engagement. This will help establish scalable, evidence-based models that strengthen early identification, reduce stigma and barriers, and promote child-centred inclusive practices aligned with national reforms.

desc: Participation on the Reference Group for the ¨Summative and formative evaluation of the out-of-school project implemented by UNICEF Venezuela¨
full:
The Education Specialist supporting the Evaluation Reference Group plays a technical advisory role throughout all phases of the evaluation, with a particular focus on educational quality, pedagogical relevance, and the coherence of the evaluative analysis of the Learning Recovery Programme for Out-of-School Children and Adolescents.

The primary responsibility of the Education Specialist is to provide specialized expertise in education, learning recovery, and educational pathways for out-of-school children and adolescents, ensuring that the evaluation:

• It is grounded in relevant pedagogical, policy, and programmatic frameworks.

• Adequately reflects the national and regional education context.

• Produces technically sound findings, conclusions, and recommendations that are relevant and actionable for decision-making.

The Education Specialist participates as an active member of the Evaluation Reference Group, contributing to the review and discussion of key consultancy deliverables (inception report, draft evaluation reports, and recommendations), engaging in technical meetings, and supporting the interpretation of results from an education-sector perspective.
The time commitment for this support is estimated at approximately two (2) working days across all stages of the evaluation.

UNICEF Zambia is also supported in integrating climate into the education programme more broadly.

TA is needed to implement key recommendations from that assessment, improve data quality, analysis, and dashboard updates, and strengthen routine data use by national and regional teams. The TA will establish a coherent data architecture, align indicator definitions and reporting frequencies, support data triangulation and dashboard rationalization, and strengthen data use for decision-making through standardized data review sessions and follow-up mechanisms. This will reduce duplication, improve data quality and consistency, and enhance use of evidence for performance monitoring, P4P, QQM, and donor reporting.

desc: Technical assistance to strengthen interoperability across DHIS2, eCHIS, and LMIS
full: Malawi’s health system increasingly relies on digital platforms to enhance data quality, reporting, and evidence-based decision-making. Key systems including DHIS2, eCHIS, and the Logistics Management Information System (LMIS) currently operate in silos, limiting interoperability, timely data sharing, and integrated planning across primary health care, supply chain, and community health services. Strengthening interoperability is critical to optimize resource allocation, support evidence-based decision-making, and improve health service delivery in alignment with HSSP III and national digital health strategies.Technical assistance to strengthen interoperability across DHIS2, eCHIS, and LMIS. The support will assist the CO in working with the MoH Digital Health Department to design and implement an integrated digital health architecture, establish data standards and interoperability protocols, align workflows across facility and community levels, and build the capacity of MoH and partner staff to maintain and use integrated systems for decision-making.

desc: Health Emergency Preparedness & Response Programme Strengthening (Risk Analysis, Coordination & Real-Time Monitoring)
full: The assistance is required to advance programmatic excellence in Health Emergency Preparedness and Response (HEPR) in 2026. The support will strengthen health risk analysis, preparedness and planning processes (EPP, HAC, HRP), and ensure high-quality emergency coordination, incident management and surge deployments. It will optimize the use of existing health information systems and real-time monitoring to enable early detection of public health incidents and data-driven, localized responses. The assistance will reinforce proactive engagement with Country and Regional Emergency Advisors for L2/L3 emergencies and ensure quality assurance and accountability across all health emergencies in coordination with humanitarian programme leads.

desc: Health Emergency Preparedness & Response (HEPR) Policy, Coordination & Medical Countermeasures Support
full: HEPR global policy alignment and advocacy: HEPR partnership development with governments, UN agencies, civil society, and community groups.Health Emergency Preparedness and Response (Public health emergency (PHE) and Health in humanitarian settings): HEPR risk assessment, preparedness, planning(EPP, HAC, HRP, etc.) for Health (including PHE).Plus operational support for emergency coordination, incident management, and surge deployments. Proactive engagement with CO/REA for L2/L3 graded emergencies and emerging threats. Quality assurance on all emergencies (Across wider Hum. Action Programm Leads).HEPR capacity strengthening for health workers, local responders, volunteers, and institutions at country and regional levels.HEPR operational support for emergency coordination, incident management, and surge deployments. Proactive engagement with CO/REA for infectious disease outbreaks.Medical Counter Measures (MCM) in Health Emergencies:Technical assistance to help countries to preprae for and respond to public health emergencies and humantarian situations by strengthen their ability to plan for and use medical countermeasures, such as vaccines, diagnostics, therapeutics, PPE and  essential health supplies. This includes making sure MCM planning is built into national Emergency Preparedness Plans, supporting countries to identify and quantify their priority needs, and helping adapt and localize approaches so they work in each context. It also includes guidance on safe and effective use, support for pre‑positioning strategies, the introduction of innovative solutions, and stronger coordination, communication, and monitoring.

desc: Domestic Financing and Immunization Sustainability Strategy Support for Malawi
full: Malawi’s primary health care system faces persistent financing challenges, with declining prioritization of immunisation and essential PHC services threatening service continuity and sustainability. Despite the Government’s commitment to Universal Health Coverage, budget allocations and execution for immunisation remain constrained by competing fiscal pressures, foreign exchange volatility, and limited medium-term expenditure planning. Strengthening domestic financing for PHC is critical to safeguard essential services and ensure sustainable vaccine delivery in line with HSSP III and the Lusaka Agenda.To address these challenges, UNICEF Malawi requests technical assistance to strengthen domestic financing for PHC, with a focus on reversing the declining prioritization of immunisation financing. The support will build on ongoing CO budget analysis work, translating fiscal evidence into actionable analytics and policy tools to influence Government of Malawi, Ministry of Health, and Treasury investment decisions. It will consolidate and deepen current PHC and immunisation financing analyses through a rapid diagnostic integrating budget trends, execution performance, and immunisation sustainability modelling. The TA will generate forward-looking scenarios for vaccine co-financing under varying macroeconomic conditions, assess delivery cost bottlenecks and value-for-money opportunities, and identify practical efficiency and reprioritization levers. Additionally, it will support the development of a budget protection framework for vaccines and essential PHC services, including earmarking and contingency financing options, medium-term expenditure projections, and concrete policy measures to strengthen immunisation prioritization within MoH budgets and the national Medium-Term Expenditure Framework.

desc: HPV Plus Strategy and Differentiated Vaccination Support for Adolescents Living with HIV (ALHIV)
full:
Zimbabwe is implementing the HPV Plus initiative under its National HPV Revitalization Strategy, approved in February 2025, which aims to raise HPV vaccination coverage to 90% by 2030. The strategy aligns with WHO guidance by introducing a differentiated schedule for adolescents living with HIV (ALHIV), who require two doses instead of the single dose given to the general population.
Despite this progress, major gaps persist in reaching ALHIV, who face a higher risk of persistent HPV infection and cervical cancer. Coverage remains particularly low among out‑of‑school adolescents and those from marginalized groups. Weak integration between HIV care services and HPV vaccination programs leads to missed opportunities to identify ALHIV and ensure completion of the two‑dose schedule. In addition, many healthcare providers lack sufficient training to deliver differentiated HPV services and tailored counselling. These challenges are further compounded by poorly integrated HIV and HPV data systems, limiting effective tracking of vaccination status and follow‑up for second doses. To operationalize the HPV Plus initiative for adolescents living with HIV (ALHIV), Zimbabwe requires targeted technical assistance to strengthen differentiated HPV vaccination delivery. Priority needs include developing standardized operational guidance and user‑friendly job aids to support implementation of the two‑dose schedule, and identifying effective service delivery platforms informed by best practices from countries with successful ALHIV HPV vaccination programs.

desc: Support the design of the HIV, teen pregnancy, and SRHR programme of the new CPD, and resource mobilization strategy.
full: Support programme design addressing HIV, teenage pregnancy, and sexual and reproductive health (SRH) among adolescent girls and young women (AGYW) following the approval of the new country programme in Eswatini. The support will focus on translating strategic priorities into integrated, evidence-based programme designs that address key drivers of vulnerability, ensure alignment with national policies and global guidance, and incorporate innovative and scalable approaches. This includes refining intervention packages, strengthening targeting and delivery modalities, and ensuring strong monitoring and learning frameworks to maximize impact for AGYW.

desc: Technical assistance to institutionalize and scale up peer-led adolescent HIV and SRHR models.
full: TA to consolidate, institutionalize, document, and scale up proven school- and community-based peer-led adolescent HIV and SRH models, particularly for girls and young mothers, while strengthening cross-sectoral monitoring, evaluation, reporting, and learning systems to drive sustainable impact and evidence-based, integrated policy and programme improvement.

desc: Last Mile Planning and EMTCT Acceleration Support for Rwanda
full:
Rwanda has made strong progress in PMTCT and maternal and child health, but persistent service delivery gaps continue to hinder the elimination of vertical transmission. To address this, the country adopted the Last Mile approach to identify and close critical bottlenecks and strengthen data-driven, targeted interventions.

This TA will support translating LM and PTE findings into a costed, coordinated national plan, enabling better resource mobilization, accountability, and accelerated progress toward achieving and sustaining triple EMTCT targets. This TA will further address follow up actions on PTE processes.

desc: EMTCT and Paediatric HIV Programme Strengthening Support for Côte d’Ivoire
full:
Côte d’Ivoire has made strong progress in PMTCT, with high antiretroviral coverage among pregnant women. However, vertical transmission has not yet been eliminated, and new pediatric HIV infections persist due to gaps in early infant diagnosis and follow-up.

Strengthening technical capacity is therefore critical to accelerate elimination of mother-to-child transmission and improve pediatric HIV outcomes. In-country technical assistance from headquarters would provide targeted, hands-on support to enhance programme quality and implementation.

desc: Gavi 6.0 Transition, VPOP and LEAP Capacity Building Support for Côte d’Ivoire
full:
Côte d’Ivoire is undergoing an accelerated transition with Gavi. With the rollout of the Gavi 6.0 strategy, new approaches and instruments, such as Gavi Leap and VPOP, are being introduced, requiring enhanced technical understanding and adaptation at country level.
To effectively align with these evolving frameworks, we aim to strengthen the capacity of our team on these new concepts and operational modalities. In this regard, we kindly request in-country technical assistance from CoE to support hands-on capacity building for staff.
Given the number of staff to be trained, sending multiple colleagues abroad would be both logistically challenging and not cost effective. An in-person mission to Côte d’Ivoire would therefore be the most efficient and impactful approach.

desc: Gavi 6.0 Strategic Planning and Proposal Development Support for Kyrgyzstan
full:
This technical assistance will provide strategic and technical support to the UNICEF CO in Kyrgyzstan and national stakeholders to enable the timely and high quality development of the country’s Gavi 6.0 proposal. The assistance will focus on clarifying the strategic direction, priorities, and scope of the forthcoming application, ensuring strong alignment with national immunization goals, health sector strategies, and Gavi 6.0 strategic principles.
The assistance will facilitate early and meaningful alignment between national immunization priorities, lessons learned from ongoing and past Gavi supported interventions, and emerging Gavi 6.0 requirements. Through structured consultations and technical inputs, the in-person mission in April 2026 will support the development of a high level conceptual framework and outline that positions the Gavi application as a coherent, holistic, and forward looking investment package.
In parallel, the mission in April will help strengthen internal CO capacity through targeted technical briefings and knowledge transfer on immunization and the Gavi portfolio. Particular emphasis will be placed on supporting the induction and strategic orientation of the new Chief of Health (TA), ensuring continuity of technical leadership and informed decision making throughout the Gavi 6.0 preparation process.
Overall, the technical assistance will enhance strategic coherence, technical quality, and institutional readiness, laying a strong foundation for a competitive and impactful Gavi 6.0 country proposal submitted by December 2026.

desc: Immunization Supply Chain Assessment, Gavi 6.0 Application and Vaccine Forecasting Support
full: 1. Malawi  needs to generate evidence-based recommendations to ensure the immunization supply chain remains adequate for current and future programme needs. Technical assistance is required to plan and conduct a comprehensive assessment to evaluate existing storage and distribution capacity at all levels. The TA will support data collection, analysis, and validation to identify functionality gaps, bottlenecks, and risks affecting vaccine potency and availability. It will also determine current and projected cold chain equipment needs based on immunization targets, new vaccine introductions, and population growth.2. As Gavi transitions into its 6.0 funding phase, Technical assistance is required to support the Ministry of Health to develop, finalize, and successfully submit a comprehensive, evidence-based Gavi 6.0 funding request for the EPI programme by the agreed deadline. The TA will coordinate partner inputs, ensure full alignment with MOH priorities, and strengthen the application through robust data analysis, realistic cost projections, and clear sustainability strategies. It will also support drafting, validation, and quality assurance to ensure the submission meets Gavi requirements and maximizes the likelihood of approval. 3. The Malawi  national EPI team need support to strengthen vaccine quantification and forecasting. The TA will support to conduct comprehensive reviews and analyses of vaccine consumption, stock, and wastage data, identify trends and gaps, and support the integration of findings into forecasting tools and future supply plans. It will also build capacity for MOH-EPI team through on-the-job mentoring and practical guidance to ensure timely, accurate, and data-driven forecasts that are fully integrated into national vaccine supply and procurement planning.

desc: Integrated Immunization Programme Review and Technical Support for Kenya
full:
The National Vaccine and Immunization Program (NVIP) of Kenya, with support from WHO and UNICEF, and in collaboration with other partner agencies, plans to conduct a comprehensive integrated programme review from 7th – 30th April 2026, which will include immunization financing, surveillance for vaccine preventable diseases (VPD), data quality, Vaccine supply and cold chain, Demand Creation, and post introduction evaluation (PIE) of TCV and IPV vaccines. The broad objective of the comprehensive review is to: assess and document progress of immunization program performance since the last comprehensive EPI (2018) review; conduct an in-depth surveillance review, examine program strengths and weaknesses and make recommendations for programme strengthening for the next medium-term period.

UNICEF is expected to provide TA/ External Reviewers for the Immunization Program Support component, among other areas.

desc: Community Feedback, Social Accountability and Immunization Demand Strengthening Support
full: Malawi continues to face  challenges related to zero-dose and missed children. Reaching zero-dose children and missed children requires an integrated primary health care (PHC) approach that address gender-related barriers, social norms, and structural barriers, while building resilient and sustained demand for immunisation services. Improved health outcomes depend on empowered communities and strengthened social accountability mechanisms that actively engage communities in participatory planning, implementation, and monitoring of health services. Building and sustaining trust in immunisation services is therefore essential and requires timely, coordinated, and evidence-based responses to community voices, concerns and satisfaction. Malawi Country Office has been collecting community feedback, rumours, and misinformation through multiple channels—both online (e.g. U-Report, media monitoring) and offline (surveys, rapid qualitative assessments, and field monitoring) but these data remain fragmented. Information is stored across different platforms and its lacks  national feedback system which consolidate, analyse, and act on community insights.There is a critical need to establish one  community feedback and response mechanism that integrates data from all existing platforms. This system will be able to do real-time analysis, coordinated response planning, and systematic use of community feedback to inform programme adjustments and policy decisions. This mechanism will support evidence-based decision-making, improve responsiveness  to community concerns and rumours, and ultimately strengthen demand for immunisation and other essential health services.

Support will also cover alignment across vaccines, target populations, and delivery platforms, ensuring that community engagement and behavioral insights are effectively applied to maximize impact and sustainability, and embedding SBC into monitoring frameworks.

desc: HPV Vaccine Uptake, Demand Generation and HPV+ Scale-Up Support
full: After the country routinized HPV vaccination, vaccine uptake has remained low, with some regions reporting rates as low as 11%. Despite availability of HPV vaccines at the health facilities, there are limited routine demand generation interventions, and resources are insufficient to reach both in school and out of school girls. Additionally, the HPV+ initiative (with support of 7% set aside fund) which has proven effective in addressing integrated issues among out of school adolescent girls has come to an end of the project implementation, and support is needed for documentation, lessons learned, and scaling up.

desc: SBC Capacity Assessment and Curriculum Development Support
full:
There is growing recognition within Viet Nam’s Ministry of Health (MOH) and other line ministries of the need for more comprehensive, system-oriented SBC strategies. However, institutional capacity for SBC research, design, implementation, and monitoring remains limited. With increasing digital connectivity, there is also a need to better integrate behavioral insights and digital engagement to enhance inclusion, equity, and sustainability.
Strengthening SBC across sectors will support more effective health and social interventions, greater community participation, and sustainable behavior change at scale.
To address this, UNICEF Viet Nam, in collaboration with MOH, is conducting a rapid organizational and capacity assessment of SBC programmes, with a focus on immunization. The assessment will identify key barriers to scale and existing capacities within the health system.
Based on the findings, UNICEF will support the development of an SBC curriculum to strengthen MOH staff capacity in planning, budgeting, managing, and monitoring SBC programmes.
UNICEF Viet Nam is seeking TA support to provide technical review, inputs, and guidance to ensure the quality of both the assessment and the curriculum development.

desc: Integrated Mother–Newborn Care Model Feasibility & Design Support (Ghana)
full: Obstetric and neonatal services are typically separated. In neonatal units, newborns are grouped together to allow healthcare workers to directly observe and treat several patients without the need to move across the ward. Mothers are hosted in a different place and can only access their babies few times per day. This is a barrier to skin-to-skin contact or early and continuous breastfeeding. A fully integrated mother and baby model of care is needed to addres maternal and newbron separation. This would be routine TA to explore the feasibility of a fully integrated maternal and newborn model of care and to design such model.

Around 3,000 community health workers play a critical role in delivering essential health and nutrition services to vulnerable mothers and children. However, the Community Health Programme, fully funded by development partners, struggles with inefficiencies that hinder its impact. Developing a national Community Health Strategy will strengthen this workforce, improve service delivery, and align efforts with global goals to save children’s lives and ensure every child grows up healthy.

The TA will also support the creation of a costed implementation plan with clear resource mapping, milestone-based performance indicators, and sustainability measures. Additionally, the TA will strengthen national governance, coordination, and accountability structures by facilitating strategy validation, securing Ministry of Health endorsement, and supporting phased dissemination and roll-out to ensure cohesive, resilient, and sustainable implementation.
