desc: Exploratory drilling and monitoring in Odweine
full: critical ground truthing needed to validate GEM based groundwater suitability maps in Somaliland’s ASAL regions, where drought and climate pressures make groundwater the most reliable water source Full details of the agreement with the Country Office can be found here - GEM_Somalia_2026.pdf

desc: Joint UNHCR-UNICEF R-WASH programme support
full:
The visit will include:
• Support and follow-on on transition management
• Consultations with Itang stakeholders. Support recovery needs, establish water services, and ensure alignment between local authorities, RRS, UNHCR, and implementing partners
• Finalising MoU and implementation modalities, including roles, sequencing, and coordination requirements for the next phase of activities.

desc: scaling Groundwater Exploration and Mapping (GEMs) to close persistent evidence gaps that limit reliable groundwater development in fragile, arid, and data poor
full: Technical support to establish a clear, replicable GEMs protocol and demonstrate how suitability mapping can better guide water supply development and reduce failed borehole drilling. It also clarifies the role of Transient Electromagnetics (TEM) in improving evidence based decision making. Full details of the agreement with the Country Office can be found here -GEM_Somalia_2026.pdf

desc: Technical support on the adoption of TEM methodology into the Afghani context
full: Support the country office with the identification of appropriate products for the context that leverage TEM methodology and can be used for gourd water projects in country. Provide technical inputs on product selection and adoption for relevant country partners.

desc: Support to test the feasibility of promoting water supply systems coupled to Managed Aquifer Recharge (MAR). while also enhancing groundwater monitoring in Kabu
full: The ACO are looking for support from the WASH innovation hub on establishing baseline monitoring, assess MAR options, and document governance and community considerations—providing actionable evidence for MAR scalability and integration into water supply systems. They also have developed three pilot sites across three Provinces of Afghanistan (Nangarhar, Herat, Kandahar), to test the feasibility of promoting water supply systems coupled to Managed Aquifer Recharge (MAR) and request support on the development of these demonstration sites and integration into national policy and best practice. Full details of the agreement with the Country Office can be found here - MAR_Afghanistan 2025.pdf  MAR_Afghanistan 2026.pdf

desc: Implementation of Groundwater Mapping and Exploration (GEMs) approach in Djibouti
full: Technical support for groundwater mapping to provide evidence and accurate information to support a proposal the Japanese Embassay for expansion of their partnership to new communities and increases the confidence of the donor to provide significant investment for expansion of the program into the rural areas of the regions of Tadjourah and ObockFull details of the agreement with the Country Office can be found here - GW Mapping_Djibouti_2025.pdf

desc: Technical support to expand Managed Aquifer Recharge (MAR) and strengthen groundwater mapping and monitoring in the Kurdistan Region of Iraq (KRI) by leveraging
full: Development of a national MAR framework, technical analysis, mapping, field validation, and positioning MAR for scale-up under government leadership. Full details of the agreement with the Country Office can be found here - MAR_Iraq_2025.pdf MAR_Iraq_2026.pdf

desc: Technical support to adopt the Transient Electromagnetic Method (TEM) for subsurface surveying, enabling precise identification of suitable groundwater recharge
full: Guidance and technical support to leverage TEM methods as part of MAR schemes to enable accurate mapping and site identification.  Full details of the agreement with the Country Office can be found here - MAR_Iraq_2025.pdf MAR_Iraq_2026.pdf

desc: Support for the introduction and adoption of TEM to enable improved drilling success rates.
full: Generate detailed operational lessons on how TEM performs in low success rate drilling environments, particularly in the context of siting boreholes for schools and healthcare facilities where failure has immediate service delivery consequences. By comparing TEM guided siting results with ongoing government drilling outcomes, the initiative will capture practical insights on data requirements, interpretation challenges, field protocols, and institutional capacities needed for successful deployment. Full details of the agreement with the Country Office can be found here -  Zimbabwe CO Agreement.pdf

desc: Support to the Government of Kenya under the More Water More Life (MWML) initiative, aimed at strengthening groundwater resilience and expanding access to safe
full: Sealing by grouting and replacement of the Banissa Borehole in Mandera County, to minimize cross aquifer contamination and safeguard groundwater quality. The Innovation Hub will support proceeding with the full grouting, rehabilitation, and associated supervision for groundwater protection and the drilling of the replacement borehole up to the freshwater aquifer depth. desc: support CO & UDAPE in measuring multidimensional child poverty (MODA)

The assistance will focus on the technical review and strengthening of the proposed methodology, including key methodological components such as the definition of dimensions, selection of indicators, weighting structure, and identification of appropriate deprivation cut-offs. The objective is to ensure that the resulting methodology is robust, policy-relevant, and aligned with international best practices, while remaining adapted to the Bolivian context and usable for national monitoring and policy design processes.

desc: Education Can't Wait proposal
full: I am reaching out to explore whether the Centre of Excellence may be able to provide direct technical surge support to South Sudan for the development of the upcoming Education Cannot Wait Multi-Year Resilience Programme (ECW-MYRP) full proposal. South Sudan has been closely engaged in the development of the MYRP process and country architecture and has played an active role in shaping the programme concept and preparing for the forthcoming call for proposals. We also just concluded the in-country mission of Dianah and the ECW team from 31 May to 5 June, which helped further clarify expectations and momentum around the next phase. We understand that the call for full proposals is expected to be launched in the second half of June. Given the timeline and complexity of the process, we are assessing how best to organize dedicated support for proposal development. We also know the compition in country for this will be very strong, with Save the Children-led consortium having been the grantee for the past 2 rounds. The full proposal will require intensive coordination and technical leadership across several streams simultaneously, including narrative development, results framework design, budgeting, consortium consultations and overall coherence of the programme package. We are currently considering a consortium structure including Oxfam, Humanity & Inclusion, and CINA as a national partner, alongside broader stakeholder engagement. At the same time, our in-country capacity is currently constrained—both within EiE and more broadly across programme teams—and we are concerned about our ability to dedicate sufficient senior technical bandwidth to lead a process of this scale while maintaining ongoing delivery. Before moving to recruit an external consultant, we wanted to check whether CoE may have availability to deploy an experienced colleague or expert to support the process in-country for approximately three weeks. Ideally, this person would lead and facilitate the proposal development process, coordinate consultations across partners, and support drafting of the full package. Someone with deep familiarity with ECW MYRP processes—and, if possible, prior experience in South Sudan or comparable fragile contexts—would be particularly valuable. We recognize this is a significant ask and timelines are short, but we thought it important to explore internal options first before launching external recruitment. Please let us know whether this could be feasible or whether there are alternative support modalities that CoE would recommend. Many thanks in advance for considering this request.

Purpose of the Request: To request technical support from the Adolescent Girls Hub to co-design and deliver a session on “Adolescent Girls as an Accelerator”, as part of the Strategic Plan and regional priorities discussions. The session will aim to: Deepen understanding of adolescent girls as a strategic accelerator within UNICEF’s Strategic Plan and the WCAR regional agenda Demonstrate how investing in adolescent girls drives multisectoral and transformative results Support translation of the accelerator approach into Country Programme Planning processes, including prioritisation and theory of change Scope of Work: Co-design and co-facilitate a 45-minute session (in collaboration with WCARO and Nigeria CO) Present global frameworks, evidence, and programmatic guidance on adolescent girls as an accelerator Illustrate linkages to Strategic Plan accelerators, gender equality priorities, and regional agenda Provide practical examples and country-relevant insights Support participants in understanding how to integrate adolescent girls into CPP components (e.g., evidence synthesis, prioritisation, ToC) Share key tools, resources, and reference materials Dates and Timing: Training Dates: 08–11 June 2026 Session Date: Monday, 8 June 2026 Time: 10:45 AM – 11:30 AM Modality: In-person (Abuja, Nigeria) Expected Outputs: Delivery of a session on Adolescent Girls as an Accelerator Strengthened participant understanding of how to apply the accelerator concept within Rights and Results Programming and CPP processes Session materials and resources shared with participants Participants: Approximately 50 participants from WCARO and Country Offices (Nigeria, Ghana, The Gambia, Guinea-Bissau) Focal Point: Takudzwa Kanyangarara

WCARO, in collaboration with OSE and participating Country Offices (Nigeria, Ghana, The Gambia, and Guinea-Bissau), is convening the Regional (Anglophone) Rights and Results Programming (RRP) Part II Training from 08–11 June 2026 in Abuja, Nigeria. The workshop aims to strengthen staff capacity to design high-quality, rights-based, and results-oriented Country Programmes, with a strong emphasis on practical application across the Country Programme Planning (CPP) cycle. As part of the agenda, a dedicated session on “Adolescent Girls as an Accelerator” is scheduled for Monday, 8 June 2026 (10:45–11:30 AM). The session will be co-facilitated by the Adolescent Girls Hub and UNICEF Nigeria Country Office and will focus on positioning adolescent girls as a strategic accelerator linked to global and regional commitments, including the UNICEF Strategic Plan, Africa Strategy, and WCAR priorities, while demonstrating their role in driving transformative, multisectoral outcomes. The Nigeria Country Office, on behalf of WCARO, is requesting technical support from the Global Gender Equality Team – Adolescent Girls Hub to virtually co-design and co-facilitate this 45-minute session. The session should present key frameworks, evidence, and practical examples, and clearly highlight how adolescent girls can be systematically integrated into Country Programme Planning processes, including evidence synthesis, prioritization, and theory of change. Expected outputs include delivery of the session and sharing of materials to support ongoing application by approximately 50 participants from WCARO and participating Country Offices. The focal point for this request is Takudzwa Kanyangarara.

desc: technical support request to strengthen he capacity of public and private (Small and Medium Entrepreneur) stakeholders in producing quality diets for children 6
full: Strengthening the capacity of public and private (Small and Medium Entrepreneur) stakeholders in producing quality diets for children 6-23 months. Given the poor diet among children in Timor-Leste and the high burden of stunting, and following the recommendations of the study on the analysis of barriers and enablers to children's diets 6-23 months, the Government is interested in investing in local and sustainable solutions to improve the quality of children's diets. In the absence of the private sector and with funding support from UNICEF Australia, UNICEF TLCO is partnering with the Tanzania Food and Nutrition Centre (TFNC) to provide technical assistance to SMEs in Timor-Leste to build their capacity to strengthen the food system for sustainable diets for children 6-23 months. Hence, support is needed to UNICEF TLCO on the food system for children, including linking with Mahidol University for nutrient analysis, generating evidence, costing and market model analysis, and reinforcing norms and standards for produced foods, and designing a scalable cash-transfer-for-child diet model. Also, the social marketing of child feeding behaviour. Expected Outcome Facilitate agreement with Mahidol University for nutrient food content analysis (Q3 2026) Establishment of standards for production of complementary foods, aligned with WHO 2023 and CODEX Guidance. (Codex Alimentarius Commission). (Q4 2026) Comprehensive First Foods market and value-chain analysis, including SME landscape, demand patterns, and affordability modelling (Q4 2026) Support on Social Marketing on feeding behaviour campaign (Q1-Q4 2026)

desc: Technical assistance to provide expert guidance on implementing strategies, designing and reviewing programs for School Age Children and Adolescents and m
full: The technical assistance will provide expert guidance on implementing strategies, designing and reviewing programs for School Age Children and Adolescents and maternal nutrition. The support will include the data analysis and triangulation for the development of a replicable programme for School Age Children and Adolescents (introduce and progressive scale-up), and review and strengthen maternal programme. Also, costing, budgeting, planning and development of a knowledge-generation agenda aligned with Timor-Leste priorities. Expected Outcome Review of nutrition interventions and indicators for school age children and adolescent (Q1 2026) Support to integration of school age children and adolescent nutrition interventions into strategies (Q3-4 2026) Review of maternal nutrition interventions (Q2 2026) Identify strategies to strengthen maternla nutrition interventions (Q3 2026)

desc: UNICEF support to TLCO MOH on MUAC simplification
full: UNICEF TLCO supported the MoH to simplify management of acute malnutrition (expand MUAC enrollment to <130mm, ration, and follow-up visit revision, exit criteria, following a MUAC/WHZ overlap analysis in 2020) which is implemented as part of primary health care to improve quality of services and leave no child behind. Currently an operational research is on-going - the results and recommendations will facilitate understanding on key performance indicators and programme efficiency. The TLCO will require technical support to finalise study, data analysis and synthesis, conduct costing, advocacy for adoption of recommendation and incorpotating the study findings into the national wasting management guideline. Also, capacity building of health worker thropugh revision of Specific Nutrition Intervention Package (SNIP) guidelines. Expected outcome Support the analysis and data synthesis, and irevision of the guidelines and indicators (Q3-4, 2026) Facilitate costing of the simplified (Q4 2026)

desc: Technica request to develop a concise, visually engaging Programme Synopsis/communication product documenting UNICEF Greece’s National Action against Childhood
full: UNICEF Greece, in partnership with the Hellenic Ministry of Health, has completed the implementation of the National Action against Childhood Obesity (2023–2025), funded through the EU Recovery and Resilience Facility. The programme encompassed primary, secondary and tertiary prevention interventions targeting children aged 0–17 and their families, spanning home, school, health services and community settings, and engaged a wide range of stakeholders including paediatricians, nutritionists, municipalities and civil society partners. With the programme cycle now concluded, UNICEF Greece is requesting Technical Assistance from the Centre of Excellence to develop a Programme Synopsis/Communication Product — a concise, visually compelling and widely disseminable knowledge product for global UNICEF audiences (including all relevant Country Offices), government counterparts, donors and partners, in coordination with the UNICEF Greece Country Office Programme and Comms team. The document should capture the key strategic shifts achieved, document core programme design parameters, highlight methodologies, tools and approaches developed and applied, and consolidate lessons learnt and good practices in a format suitable for replication in other national contexts. The product will serve as a key outcome document for dissemination through UNICEF's ICON platform and other global knowledge-sharing channels, contributing to UNICEF's broader evidence base on national-level childhood obesity prevention.

desc: Gavi 6.0 Application Development, Costing and Quality Assurance Support
full: Short Description: Technical assistance to support the planning, development, and quality assurance of the Gavi 6.0 grant application, including alignment with national immunization priorities, development of investment cases, costing inputs, and preparation of high-quality programmatic and financial components (e.g., CashPOP, VPOP), in line with Gavi 6.0 requirements and timelines. Objectives and Expectations Overall Objective To ensure the timely development and submission of a high-quality, evidence-based, and fully costed Gavi 6.0 application that is aligned with national priorities and maximizes impact for zero-dose and under-immunized children. Specific Objectives Provide technical guidance for application design, structure, and compliance with Gavi 6.0 requirements. Support the development/refinement of the investment case, including prioritization of high-impact interventions. Strengthen integration of NIS strategies and zero-dose agenda within the application. Support realistic costing and financial articulation, including CashPOP and VPOP components. Facilitate stakeholder alignment and consensus-building across government and partners. Ensure quality assurance through iterative review and alignment with global best practices. Expected Deliverables Draft and final Gavi 6.0 application package, including all required sections. Costed workplans and budgets, aligned with Gavi guidance and national systems. Completed VPOP and/or CashPOP sections, with financial justification. Review reports and feedback notes from technical experts at key milestones. Facilitated workshops/consultations (virtual or in-country) to support joint planning and validation. Final quality assurance check prior to submission

desc: VPOP Application and Gavi 6.0 Prioritization Support
full: Short Description of TA Request Request for technical assistance to support the application of the Vaccine Prioritization and Optimization (VPOP) approach within the Gavi 6.0 application Overall Objective To ensure that the Gavi 6.0 application is strategically focused, evidence-based, and optimized through the application of VPOP, maximizing impact and value for money in reaching missed populations. Specific Objectives Support the application of VPOP methodology to prioritize high-impact immunization interventions. Ensure alignment between prioritized interventions, NIS strategies, and funding envelopes. Facilitate evidence-based decision-making through data review and scenario analysis. Support documentation and integration of VPOP outputs into the Gavi 6.0 application. Build country capacity in prioritization and optimization approaches for sustainability. Expected Deliverables VPOP analysis outputs (including criteria, assumptions, and prioritization rationale) Scenario analyses showing trade-offs and optimization options Integration of VPOP results into the Gavi application narrative and workplan Facilitated prioritization workshops/decision-making session Final quality review of VPOP components within the application

desc: UNICEF Afganistan Nutrition is integration of climate resilience within nutrition programming. The workshop will bring together participants from NGOs, UN agenc
full: UNICEF Afganistan Nutrition is integration of climate resilience within nutrition programming. The workshop will bring together participants from NGOs, UN agencies, development partners, and relevant sectors such as Nutrition, Health, and WASH to promote a multisectoral approach to addressing climate-related risks affecting nutrition outcomes. With the recent rollout of the First Food Initiative in Afghanistan, this workshop will also explore opportunities to link First Food approaches with climate-resilient and climate-sensitive programming in the country context. To support this effort, we are requesting facilitation support from a senior Nutrition Advisor / technical experts from the Centre of Excellence (CoE) in the relevant thematic area. The support will include planning and execution of the workshop, specifically conduct a desk review of key country-level documents and providing in-person facilitation of the 3-day training. The Senior advisor/technical expert will help strengthen technical content, guide cross-sector discussions, and contribute to the development of practical recommendations and action points to enhance climate-resilient and nutrition-sensitive programming in Afghanistan.

desc: Develop Disability inclusive budgeting guidelines
full:
This request seeks technical assistance to support the Government of Kenya to develop and operationalize disability inclusive budgeting guidelines that are aligned with national public finance systems, building on recent workshop outcomes that focused on integrating disability into budget cycles, strengthening tagging approaches, and aligning with global commitments such as the Amman Berlin Declaration and the 15 percent target. The support will strengthen systems for planning, allocating, tracking, and reporting disability inclusive expenditure by providing a technical review and consolidation of draft guidelines, ensuring alignment with PFM frameworks, IFMIS and SCOA structures, and international tagging standards while defining clear criteria for disability specific and mainstreamed spending across sectors. It will deliver revised guidelines, an operational framework for integration into the budget cycle, a harmonized tagging methodology, capacity development materials, and an implementation roadmap, while engaging relevant global expertise across public finance, disability inclusion, social protection, and monitoring systems. Through the global support, learning and technical engagement, the request will leverage strategic accelerators including existing tagging systems, alignment with the 2027 to 2028 budget cycle, adaptation of gender and child budgeting practices, strong government ownership, structured engagement with organizations of persons with disabilities, and coordinated support from development partners to ensure high quality and rapidly implementable results.

Specifically, the support relates to a mission to contribute to two workshops between 24 and 26 June engaging a range of national stakeholders on these topics.

full: We initiated this process for the “Evaluation of the National Fund for Children and Adolescents with Disabilities” prior to the Panama Centre of Excellence becoming operational; as such, it was not possible to follow the standard procedure for the use of the Long-Term Agreement (LTA) in this instance. In this regard, we kindly seek your validation prior to proceeding further with the process. We would highly appreciate your feedback and remain available to address any comments or questions you may have. With greater clarity now on the applicable procedures and on the colleagues within the Centre of Excellence (CoE) with whom we should coordinate regarding the use of the LTA, the Office will ensure full adherence to the established process in future consultancies.

desc: Climate Resilience, Climate Action and Sustainability Training for ZCO
full: TA Request: Climate Resilience, Climate Action and Sustainability Training for ZCO Short Description of the Request This Technical Assistance (TA) request seeks to design and facilitate a tailored two-session training for UNICEF Zambia Country Office (ZCO) staff on Module 2 in Climate Resilience, Climate Action, and Sustainability. The training will strengthen staff capacity to integrate climate-responsive programming, risk-informed planning, and sustainability principles across sectors. The training will be delivered over two half-day sessions: 29 July (09:00–12:00) 30 July (14:00–17:00) It will combine practical tools, global best practices, and Zambia-specific contextualization to ensure applicability to ongoing and future programmes. After completing Module 2, learners will be better able to: Co-create, with colleagues from multiple sectors: 1) a causality flowchart for the country, tracing climate change impacts on children and youth, and 2) a product to help mainstream climate resilience and action with and for children, e.g. an infographic, resource list, narrative Describe the difference between primary, secondary and cascading hazards and impacts Research the climate risks and hazards in your country using online resources Give sectoral examples of direct and indirect climate impacts on children and youth Explain how children experience the impacts of climate change disproportionately by describing at least four types of their distinct vulnerabilities Explain how adolescent girls are particularly exposed to climate and environmental hazards considering the underlying vulnerabilities they live with Use multiple sources to discover and describe opportunities for children and youth in your country to be agents of change in climate resilience and action, e.g. breaking the harmful vulnerability-exposure loop, taking leadership roles in climate governance and finance Analyze a participation opportunity for children and youth for its level of engagement Identify key stakeholders and partners to consult with on climate resilience and climate action

desc: CC4C: Development of an Adolescent Climate Action Engagement Strategy
full: Request for Technical Support: Development of an Adolescent Climate Action Engagement Strategy UNICEF Malawi is seeking technical support from the Centre of Excellence to develop an Adolescent Climate Action Engagement Strategy that will provide a coherent framework for adolescent engagement in climate action programming. Climate change is increasingly affecting the lives and futures of adolescents in Malawi, particularly adolescent girls who face heightened vulnerabilities related to education, health, livelihoods, and protection. In response, UNICEF Malawi has been supporting a range of climate action initiatives involving both in-school and out-of-school adolescents. These interventions have included climate awareness raising, climate-smart livelihoods, environmental conservation activities, leadership development, advocacy, and adolescent participation in climate-related platforms. While these efforts have generated valuable results, they have evolved through different programme streams and partnerships without an overarching strategic framework to guide and harmonize adolescent climate engagement. As a result, there is a need to develop a clear strategy that articulates UNICEF Malawi's vision, priorities, approaches, and expected results for adolescent climate action. The proposed technical support would assist UNICEF Malawi to: • Review existing adolescent climate action initiatives and lessons learned; • Assess opportunities and gaps in adolescent engagement in climate action and resilience-building; • Define a strategic framework for engaging adolescents, with particular attention to adolescent girls and other vulnerable groups; • Identify evidence-based approaches for strengthening adolescent leadership, participation, advocacy, and climate resilience; • Develop a results framework and implementation roadmap; • Ensure alignment with UNICEF's global and regional climate strategies, the Malawi Country Programme, and national climate policies and priorities. The strategy will support UNICEF Malawi in moving from fragmented climate-related activities towards a more intentional, evidence-based, and scalable approach to adolescent climate action, positioning adolescents as agents of change while strengthening their resilience to climate-related shocks and stresses.

desc: Measles Outbreak Root Cause Analysis and Immunization Systems Strengthening Support
full: Technical Assistance Request: Capacity Building and Root Cause Analysis for Measles Outbreaks 1. Title Capacity Building of UNICEF Staff and Facilitation of Root Cause Analysis (RCA) for Measles Outbreaks in High-Burden States 2. Background and Justification In 2026, India has experienced high burden of Measles cases and outbreaks. Within the first five months of 2026, more than 19,800 cases of measles have been reported across the country which have surpassed the total number of 17,800 cases reported in the entire year of 2025. With high reported administrative coverages of MCV, these cases and outbreaks highlight persistent gaps in routine immunization systems, surveillance, and outbreak response capacity. Despite ongoing immunization efforts, outbreaks continue to occur due to a combination of systemic, programmatic, and contextual factors. Root Cause Analysis (RCA) is a structured approach that helps identify underlying causes contributing to outbreaks, beyond surface-level factors. Strengthening the capacity of UNICEF staff at country and field offices to conduct RCA will enable systematic identification of bottlenecks in immunization service delivery, surveillance, demand generation, and program management. Furthermore, conducting RCA exercises in high-burden states will generate context-specific evidence to inform targeted action plans. These action plans are expected to strengthen immunization systems and contribute to reducing the burden of vaccine-preventable diseases, particularly measles. 3. Objectives General Objective To strengthen the capacity of UNICEF staff and support evidence-based action planning through Root Cause Analysis of measles outbreaks. Specific Objectives Build technical capacity of UNICEF country office and field office staff to plan and conduct RCA of measles outbreaks. Facilitate comprehensive RCA exercises in two states with high measles burden in 2026. Support development of actionable, evidence-based plans to strengthen immunization systems. Promote integration of RCA findings into routine immunization and outbreak response strategies. 4. Scope of Work The TA will support the following key activities: 4.1 Capacity Building Develop/adapt RCA training materials tailored to the immunization context. Conduct training workshop for UNICEF staff (Health & SBC teams) at country and field levels. Provide practical guidance on RCA tools, methodologies, and data interpretation. 4.2 Facilitation of RCA in Two High-Burden States Facilitate planning and execution of RCA exercises, including: Data review (surveillance, immunization coverage, outbreak response) Stakeholder consultations (health officials, frontline workers, communities) Field assessments Apply standardized RCA frameworks (e.g., fishbone analysis, problem tree analysis). 4.3 Development of Action Plans Support state teams to translate RCA findings into targeted action plans. Ensure plans address key system gaps (e.g., service delivery, cold chain, demand generation, data quality). Define clear timelines, responsibilities, and monitoring indicators. 4.4 Documentation and Knowledge Sharing Document RCA findings, lessons learned, and best practices. Develop a summary report and guidance note for wider dissemination. 5. Expected Deliverables Adapted RCA training package and tools. Capacity-building sessions conducted for UNICEF staff. RCA reports for two high-burden states. State-specific action plans to strengthen immunization systems. Final TA report summarizing findings, recommendations, and lessons learned. 6. Timeline Proposed duration: 3–4 months Activity Timeline Preparation and tool adaptation Weeks 1–2 Capacity building workshops Week 2 RCA in two states Weeks 3-5 Action plan development Weeks 5–7 Reporting and dissemination Weeks 8-10 7. Resource Requirements Technical expert(s) with experience in: Immunization systems strengthening Measles outbreak investigation Root Cause Analysis methodologies Budget for: Training workshops (travel, venue, materials) Field visits for data collection in two states Workshops for development of action plans Documentation and reporting 8. Expected Outcomes Enhanced capacity of UNICEF staff to independently conduct RCA. Improved understanding of drivers of measles outbreaks in high-burden states. Development of targeted, evidence-based interventions. Strengthened immunization systems, contributing to reduced incidence of measles and other vaccine-preventable diseases. 9. Monitoring and Evaluation Number of staff trained on RCA methodologies. Completion of RCA exercises in selected states. Quality and implementation status of developed action plans. Evidence of integration of RCA findings into program strategies. 10. Request for Technical Assistance UNICEF ICO seeks technical support to: Design and deliver RCA capacity-building activities. Facilitate RCA exercises in identified states. Guide development of actionable immunization strengthening plans.

full: Regarding the programming for adolescent boys. At SoP CO, we’ve recently wrapped up empowering sessions for adolescent girls and young women, which have been received very well by our partners. Now, we’re turning our attention to discovering and developing programming for adolescent boys, especially considering the high risks they face related to violence in Gaza and the West Bank. Tunisia Country Office's partner the Tunisian Association for the Defence of the RIghts of the Child is organising a major event in Tunisia on 16 June wtih a Coalition of African Civil Society Organisations for the Defence of the Rights of African Children (CADE). This will mobilize some 200 CSOs from 12 countries, as well as Parliamentarians from various countries as well as from Tunisia. The event will focus on FGM and Early Marriage and is seen by Tunisia Country Office as a great opportunity to support civil society more generally and child rights specifically. We very much would like the coalition to be successful and are providing financial support for the organisation of the hybrid event, whilst seeking support from the CEO for technical expertise both for the panels and for the review and roll-out of the planned advocacy action plan. Mobilised colleagues are Line Rasmussen and Ellen Alem. Kindly officially asking for their support and expertise on these topics which fall outside of the expertise of the country offfice, while noting also that the coalition's success will certainly be of great added value for UNICEF's advocacy for child rights more broadly.

desc: Lebanon is facing a severe humanitarian crisis with widespread displacement, affecting children and caregivers' access to nutrition, early childhood development
full: Lebanon is facing a severe and escalating humanitarian crisis, with the most recent shocks resulting in the displacement of an estimated 10–20 per cent of the population. Many displaced families are now living in precarious and temporary accommodation, including collective shelters, tents, unfinished or overcrowded rented housing, and with host families. These conditions have had a profound impact on children and caregivers, severely disrupting diets, access to essential nutrition and early childhood development (ECD) services, and caregiving practices for infants, young children, adolescent girls, and pregnant and breastfeeding women. Widespread displacement, economic hardship, and insecurity are placing intense pressure on families’ ability to provide adequate nutrition and nurturing care. Children are growing up in environments characterized by instability, overcrowding, limited privacy, and poor conditions for food preparation, storage, hygiene, play, and early stimulation. Heightened caregiver stress, poverty, repeated disruption, and the erosion of family and community support systems are increasing risks of malnutrition, inappropriate feeding practices, and long‑term developmental harm. Caregivers face mounting barriers, including limited access to diverse and nutritious foods; unsafe and unsolicited donations of breastmilk substitutes and ultra‑processed foods; and reduced opportunities for breastfeeding, responsive feeding, and nurturing care. Disruptions to routine health, nutrition, and ECD services further undermine families’ capacity to sustain appropriate diets and caregiving practices during this period of acute vulnerability. In response, UNICEF, in close coordination with the Ministry of Public Health (MoPH) and Nutrition Sector partners, is implementing an integrated, life‑cycle nutrition response to protect child survival, growth, and development. This includes infant and young child feeding (IYCF) and ECD counselling, wasting screening and treatment, preventive and emergency nutrition supplies, nutrition risk communication, protection of breastfeeding through regulation of breastmilk substitutes, and nutrition surveillance, delivered through health, community, education, and digital platforms. As the Nutrition Sector/Cluster lead and main technical partner for nutrition and ECD in Lebanon, UNICEF provides technical leadership, strengthens frontline capacity, ensures continuity of essential supplies, supports coordination and information management, and maintains last‑resort service delivery where gaps persist. Within this context, short‑term technical assistance is required to support the operationalization of new capacity‑strengthening roles, consolidate nutrition education across the life course, develop initial technical guidance for emerging response modalities, and strengthen nutrition advocacy and resource mobilization to support the strategic design, coherence, and operational readiness of the integrated Nutrition and Child Development emergency response in a rapidly evolving crisis context with particular emphasis on emergency financing, sector coordination, and technical guidance for emerging response modalities.

desc: Support the Ministry of Social Development in revising and strengthening a proposal to create a Care Directorate
full: Description: Request TA to support the Ministry of Social Development in revising and strengthening a proposal to create a Care Directorate, including its organizational model, programmatic offer, and an analysis of Latin American care experiences to inform its design. Also, support is needed for a technical peer review of a social protection strategy document to enhance quality, technical coherence, and alignment with international standards. Objectives & Expectations: Strengthen the Care Directorate’s proposal by integrating comparative Latin American care experiences into its organizational model and programmatic offer. Improve the social protection strategy document through technical peer review. Ensure alignment with international standards and best practices in care systems and social protection. Deliverables: Revised proposal for the Care Directorate (organizational model + programmatic offer + Latin American care experiences analysis). Peer-reviewed social protection strategy document with recommendations. Other Global Practices to Involve: Social Protection Gender Equality & Inclusion Health & Nutrition (as relevant to care systems) Strategic Accelerators: Going to scale (to ensure the Care Directorate model is designed for national expansion and impact) Gender and youth-responsive lens to deliver accelerated, inclusive results for children

Dear colleagues, our office would like to submit a HIP proposal https://en.innovasjonnorge.no/ we are currently identifying the exact sector, but we were told it would be beneficial to already submit a TA so that we can ensure we will get a spot on the submissions that the organisation can do. This is my first submission, but as Nour Alanjjar has been supporting us I was wondering if this TA could be allocated to Linus.

UNICEF Benin is seeking technical support from an expert in Social and Behaviour Change (SBC) and Human-Centered Design (HCD) to help refine and strengthen child marriage prevention strategies. Benin has made significant progress in reducing child marriage through policy reforms and programmes such as the Cash Plus Care initiative, which has combined social protection, support for girls' school retention, and SBC interventions to address harmful social norms. However, child marriage remains prevalent among certain agro-pastoral communities, suggesting that existing approaches may not sufficiently address the specific drivers, aspirations, constraints, and decision-making dynamics within these groups. UNICEF would therefore like to explore the use of a Human-Centered Design approach to better understand the lived experiences of adolescent girls, families, and community leaders, identify persistent bottlenecks to behaviour change, and co-create contextually appropriate solutions that can inform a renewed and more effective strategy for preventing child marriage in these communities.

desc: Ensure quality for self-supply water in schools
full:
Dear colleagues,
The Ministry of Health is currently developing a circular on the implementation of school health programmes and has requested UNICEF’s support in this process.
One key issue that has emerged is how the circular should provide practical guidance on monitoring and ensuring water quality for schools and communities that rely on self-managed water supply systems.
We would appreciate guidance and technical input from CoE colleagues on relevant approaches, standards, and good practices that could inform this section of the circular.

Thank you very much for your support.

desc: National survey on WASH and health policy in schools
full:
Dear colleagues,
UNICEF, in collaboration with WHO and MoET, is conducting a national survey on WASH and school health policies and practices. The draft report is expected to be available in August, and a dissemination workshop is tentatively planned for September/October 2026
We would greatly appreciate your support in the following areas:
- Reviewing and providing comments on the draft report and policy brief.
- Providing suggestions for the national workshop to disseminate the survey findings and support policy advocacy on financing for WASH.
Thank you very much for your support.
Minh
